verdicts.reasoning: Hands-on QuickBooks training for small business accounting aligns directly with the Business/Management and Finance career clusters under OSPI's Business & Marketing program area. Course delivers occupation-specific software competencies (general ledger, AP/AR, inventory, invoicing, payroll) tied to industry-standard accounting practices, with applied performance-based learning rather than academic theory. Strong fit for Preparatory CTE with potential CTSO linkage to FBLA/DECA.

Payroll Accounting is an occupation-specific (Preparatory) course aligned to the Finance career cluster and OSPI Business & Marketing program area. Content (FICA/FUTA withholding, payroll records, tax form preparation) maps directly to industry payroll-specialist competencies and credentials (FPC, Certified Bookkeeper). Applied technical skill demonstrations (computing wages, preparing 941/W-2) satisfy CTE performance-assessment requirements, and the course supports FBLA/DECA accounting CTSO linkage. Clear workforce-need justification for bookkeeping/payroll roles. Properly classified as CTE.

Federal Income Tax aligns with the Finance career cluster and Business & Marketing OSPI program area. It delivers occupation-specific technical competencies (1040 preparation, tax law application, schedule completion) that map to recognized industry credentials (IRS AFSP, enrolled agent pathway, VITA certification) and accounting/tax-preparer career pathways. Content is preparatory, applied, and performance-based (preparing actual tax returns), distinguishing it from general academic economics. Fits squarely within Business/Finance CTE.

Governmental Accounting aligns with the Finance and Business/Management career clusters under OSPI's Business & Marketing program area. It delivers occupation-specific technical competencies (fund accounting, GASB-style reporting for governmental/proprietary/fiduciary funds) that map to industry accounting standards and progress toward recognized credentials (CPA pathway, government accounting roles). It is preparatory CTE content, not general-ed academic transfer, and supports FBLA/DECA CTSO linkage.

Business Communications aligns directly with the Business Management & Administration national career cluster and OSPI's Business & Marketing program area. Course delivers occupation-specific technical competencies (business letters, memos, e-mails, written/oral reports, word-processing and presentation-graphic software) applied in real-world business contexts. Embeds employability/21st century skills (ethical communication, professional document creation, oral presentation delivery) central to CTE Core Employability Skills. Performance-based assessments are inherent (produced documents and delivered presentations rather than solely exams). Bates Technical College is a CTE-focused institution and this is a foundational course in Business pathways with FBLA CTSO linkage potential. Distinct from general-ed CMST&/ENGL& speech/composition because it is explicitly framed around business industry contexts and software tools, not academic transfer rhetoric.

Project-based marketing course aligns with the Marketing career cluster and OSPI's Business & Marketing program area. CIP 52.0201 (Business Administration/Management) supports CTE classification. Content (creating, communicating, delivering value offerings) maps to industry marketing competencies and supports DECA/FBLA CTSO integration. Project-based delivery satisfies performance assessment requirement rather than relying solely on exams.

Digital Marketing Strategy aligns with the Marketing career cluster and OSPI Business & Marketing program area. CIP 52.0201 (Business Administration/Management) supports CTE classification. Course is project-based with applied digital marketing competencies (social media, paid advertising, earned media) consistent with industry-standard marketing practices and employability skills. CTSO linkage available via DECA/FBLA. Preparatory framing toward marketing/business pathway.

CIP 52.0201 (Business Administration/Management) aligns to Business/Management career cluster and OSPI Business & Marketing program area. Course content (managing teams, leading through disruption, risk management) delivers occupational competencies in management pathway with applied leadership/employability skills relevant to FBLA/DECA CTSO alignment. Fits Preparatory CTE framing rather than pure academic transfer.

Project-based SEO course aligns with Marketing and Business/Management clusters under OSPI's Business & Marketing program area. CIP 52.0201 (Business Administration/Management) supports CTE classification. Course delivers occupational digital marketing competencies with applied/project-based assessments rather than pure academic content, fitting Preparatory CTE framing with FBLA/DECA CTSO potential.

HRM principles align with Business/Management cluster (CIP 52.0201), OSPI Business & Marketing program area. Covers strategic people management, applied HR practices, and employability skills relevant to workforce. Preparatory course tied to business pathway; supports SHRM-aligned competencies.

Employment Law (CIP 52.0201 - Business Administration/Management) aligns with Business/Management career cluster and OSPI Business & Marketing program area. Course covers employer/employee relationships, labor law, and regulatory compliance - directly applicable to HR, business management, and supervisory occupational pathways. Supports preparatory CTE framing with industry-relevant legal/compliance competencies and employability skills (workplace rights, regulatory understanding). FBLA CTSO alignment is natural. Keep as CTE.

Small Business Planning aligns with Business/Management & Administration career cluster and OSPI Business & Marketing program area. CIP 52.0201 (Business Administration/Management) is an OSPI-recognized CTE CIP. Course delivers occupational competencies (market analysis, value positioning, operational/financial structures) with a performance assessment (actionable business plan and marketing strategy), supporting Preparatory designation and FBLA/DECA CTSO linkage. Embeds employability/21st century skills via highly interactive applied work.

Applied Business Capstone (CIP 52.0201 Business Administration/Management) aligns with the Business Management & Administration national career cluster and OSPI's Business & Marketing program area. Project-based capstone synthesizing programmatic outcomes with specialization track demonstrates preparatory CTE characteristics: performance assessment via project demonstration, application of industry-relevant business competencies, and integration of employability skills in real-world business contexts. CIP 52.0201 is a recognized CTE business administration code. Keep CTE classification.

Course aligns with Business/Management and IT career clusters with applied financial/business competencies (budgeting, cost-benefit, resource allocation, software cost estimation) directly tied to IT project management, governance, and leadership roles — clear occupational/preparatory framing rather than general-ed economics or finance theory.

Early Childhood Practicum II aligns directly with OSPI CTE standards: Education & Training career cluster and FCS program area. Preparatory field experience delivers occupation-specific competencies (lesson planning, ASQ child observation using industry-standard assessment, family conferencing) with performance-based assessment by on-site supervisor in real-world early learning context. Supports progression toward recognized credentials (CDA, ECE certificate, WA STARS). Includes embedded employability/communication skills. Clear preparatory CTE designation, not general academic transfer.

Early Childhood Education Professionalism aligns with the Education & Training career cluster and Family & Consumer Sciences (FCS) OSPI program area. The course explicitly covers profession's code of ethics, advocacy for children/families, professional portfolio development, and resource files on professional publications/organizations — these are core CTE Leadership & Employability Skills components applied in a real-world ECE industry context. ECE is a recognized CTE pathway leading to credentials (CDA, Initial/Short ECE Certificate, AAS) with NAEYC professional standards alignment. Portfolio creation is a performance assessment, not just an exam. Preparatory designation tied to ECE CIP. Keep as CTE.

Practicum in early learning settings at a technical college; aligns with Education & Training cluster, Preparatory ECE pathway. Includes industry-aligned assessment (ASQ), real-world performance demonstrations, family communication employability skills, and progression toward recognized ECE credentials (WA STARS, ECE certificates). Classic Preparatory CTE.

ECE211 at Bates Technical College is a core course in the Early Childhood Education preparatory pathway, aligning to the Education & Training career cluster and OSPI's Family & Consumer Sciences program area. Content (child development factors, self-concept, temperament, social-emotional milestones, pro-social behavior) maps to NAEYC professional standards and WA Early Achievers/MERIT competencies required for licensed childcare/preschool workforce. Bates is a technical college whose ECE sequence leads to recognized postsecondary credentials (State ECE Initial Certificate, Short Certificate, AAS), satisfying the preparatory CTE requirement of progression toward industry credentials. FCCLA serves as the aligned CTSO. Documented WA childcare workforce shortage provides skills-gap justification. Course is occupation-specific (not general academic transfer like PSYC& 200), so it satisfies CTE rather than general electives.

Early Childhood Education course aligns with Education & Training career cluster and FCS program area. Covers occupational competencies (DAP, CLAD, cognitive milestones, STEM activity design) directly tied to early childhood educator pathway with industry-recognized standards (NAEYC, WA STARS). Includes performance-based assessments (create/demonstrate/evaluate activities, develop tools) rather than solely exams. Supports progression toward CDA credential and ECE stackable certificates.

ECE213 is part of Bates Technical College's Early Childhood Education program, which aligns directly with the Education & Training career cluster and OSPI's FCS program area. The course teaches occupational competencies (teaching strategies, developmentally appropriate activity planning/implementation for children birth-age 8) tied to NAEYC industry standards and state ECE credentialing pathways (CDA, Early Achievers). Content is preparatory and applied — students plan and implement creative activities rather than studying art history academically — fitting the Preparatory CTE designation under CIP 19.0708. FCCLA CTSO alignment is available. This is not a fine-arts appreciation course; it is a pedagogy/methods course for a recognized workforce pathway, so CTE classification is appropriate.

Early Childhood Education course aligns with Education & Training career cluster (one of 16 national clusters) and OSPI's Family & Consumer Sciences program area. NAEYC standards alignment provides the industry/nationally-defined technical standards required for CTE. ECE coursework at Bates is part of an occupation-preparatory pathway toward ECE certificates/credentials (CDA, state ECE certifications), satisfying the postsecondary credential progression requirement. Applied instructional strategies and experiential learning satisfy performance-assessment expectations. Properly classified as CTE.

Bates ECE216 is part of Bates Technical College's Early Childhood Education program, which is a Skilled & Technical Sciences CTE program aligned to the Education & Training career cluster. The course addresses diversity, inclusion, anti-bias curriculum design, and self-reflection for future educators — core competencies in ECE workforce preparation. ECE coursework at Bates ladders into stackable credentials (Washington State Initial/Short/State ECE Certificates aligned with MERIT and DCYF requirements), satisfying the recognized postsecondary credential progression required by OSPI. Content aligns with NAEYC professional preparation standards (industry-defined standards for the cluster). Appropriate to keep as CTE.

Work-based learning/cooperative work experience tied to a student's program of study directly aligns with CTE: integrates classroom learning with industry-context work, requires written training agreement with employer-defined competencies, and explicitly targets employability skills and career evaluation. ECE prefix indicates Early Childhood Education pathway (Education & Training cluster, Human Services adjacent). Matches OSPI Preparatory CTE criteria with real-world industry application and performance-based assessment.

EMT course aligns directly with Health Science career cluster and Skilled & Technical Sciences OSPI program area. Includes industry-standard curriculum (NREMT certification), hands-on labs, field internships (real-world business/industry context), patient assessment and trauma care competencies, plus decision-making/communication (employability skills). Leads to a recognized postsecondary credential (NREMT). Preparatory CTE course - textbook fit.

Engineering Technology Study Lab I is a cohort-based support lab tied directly to the engineering technology program at Bates (a technical college). The course provides contextualized lab projects spanning the first quarter's engineering coursework, plus career preparation, college navigation, and soft skills (coping with pressure, decision making) - which align with CTE Core Employability Skills and 21st Century Skills integration required by OSPI. The lab is embedded in the STEM/Skilled & Technical Sciences cluster (engineering technology pathway) at a technical college, supporting occupational competencies and progression toward a recognized credential. While it has a student-success flavor (financial aid, library services, workforce funding), it is tethered to a specific CTE program cohort and engineering coursework rather than being a generic college-success seminar. Conservative call: keep CTE.

Engineering Technology Study Lab II is a cohort-based lab supporting contextualized engineering projects with explicit career preparation, employment-focused social media profile creation, and soft skills training (drive for results, cooperative teamwork). This aligns with the STEM/Engineering & Technology cluster and integrates Core Employability Skills and 21st Century Skills in a real-world preparatory context, consistent with OSPI CTE Skilled & Technical Sciences / STEM program area requirements.

Engineering Technology Study Lab III directly aligns with CTE standards: cohort-based lab setting with contextualized engineering projects (STEM/Skilled & Technical Sciences cluster), explicit Core Employability Skills integration (initiative, flexibility soft skills), and career preparation activities (cover letters, resumes, mock interviews) that map to OSPI's required Leadership & Employability Skills strand. The course is part of an engineering technology preparatory sequence (third quarter, progressing toward graduation), satisfies the performance-assessment requirement through lab projects rather than exams, and supports progression toward a recognized postsecondary/workforce credential. This is a textbook Preparatory CTE support lab.

Engineering Technology study lab tied to a cohort-based engineering program with contextualized projects, career preparation (internships, industry networking, industry discussions), and explicit employability/soft skill development (influential communication, continuous learning). Aligns with STEM/Skilled & Technical Sciences cluster as a preparatory pathway support course with leadership/employability integration and real-world industry engagement.

Engineering Technology Study Lab supporting a cohort-based engineering program sequence; explicitly STEM/Engineering cluster aligned, embeds career prep, employability/soft skills (decision-making, strategic vision), and transition to transfer/employer applications — consistent with Preparatory CTE lab support within Skilled & Technical Sciences/STEM program area.

Bates ENGR196 is a cohort-based engineering technology lab tied to a sequenced engineering program (sixth quarter), with contextualized projects, career preparation (resumes, cover letters, professional social media), and explicit employability/soft skills ("planning and organizing," "integrity and respect"). This aligns with the STEM / Skilled & Technical Sciences cluster, integrates CTE Core Employability and Leadership Skills in real-world contexts, and supports progression toward a recognized engineering technology credential — core CTE strands.

Work-based Learning Experience is a hallmark CTE preparatory course — directly aligns curriculum to industry/workplace tasks in a career field (engineering cluster), embeds real-world employability skills, and bridges program-of-study competencies to occupational application. This is exactly the Preparatory CTE model OSPI endorses (worksite learning / supervised industry experience).

Operations and Supply Chain Essentials aligns directly with the Transportation, Distribution & Logistics national Career Cluster and OSPI's Business & Marketing / Skilled & Technical Sciences program areas. Content covers logistics, warehouse management, operations processes, and industry-responsive adaptation — core occupational competencies in the TDL cluster. Course is appropriately exploratory/preparatory for supply chain pathways linked to recognized credentials (e.g., APICS/ASCM CSCP, CLA). Keep CTE.

Apprenticeship-context course (LOG = logging/apprentice program at Bates) explicitly framed for workplace communication, technical writing, business communications, team/intercultural communications, conflict management, and professional portfolio with resume and work samples. Maps to Core Employability Skills and 21st Century Skills strand within a Skilled & Technical Sciences apprenticeship pathway. Performance assessment (portfolio) is consistent with CTE expectations. Not a general-ed CMST course; apprenticeship framing supplies the cluster alignment.

Lean Six Sigma Foundations aligns directly with the Manufacturing career cluster (and Transportation/Distribution/Logistics) under OSPI's Skilled & Technical Sciences program area. Course delivers industry-recognized technical competencies (DMAIC process, value stream analysis, waste identification, key metrics) tied to nationally defined Lean Six Sigma standards used by ASQ and IISE. Includes leadership/employability skills (team leadership tools, stakeholder communication, presenting data to co-workers) applied in real-world business/industry contexts. Progresses toward recognized postsecondary credentials (Six Sigma Yellow/Green Belt certification). Performance-based with data analysis and process-improvement demonstrations rather than purely academic exams.

Inventory and Warehouse Management aligns directly with the Transportation, Distribution & Logistics national career cluster and OSPI's Business & Marketing / Skilled & Technical Sciences program areas. Content (inventory types, financial accounting of inventory, barcodes/RFID automation, supply chain risk management) maps to industry-recognized logistics standards (e.g., MSSC CLA, APICS/ASCM CSCP) and delivers occupation-specific technical competencies rather than general academic content. Course is preparatory in nature and supports progression toward recognized logistics/supply chain credentials.

Enterprise Resource Planning aligns clearly with the Business/Management and Transportation/Distribution/Logistics career clusters under OSPI's Business & Marketing and Skilled & Technical Sciences program areas. Course delivers occupation-specific competencies (ERP software use, procurement, demand management, invoicing, import/export documentation, cross-border shipping) tied to industry-standard logistics and supply chain practices. Content is preparatory, technical, and directly maps to recognized postsecondary logistics/SCM credentials. Not a general academic transfer course.

Principles of Accounting I aligns with the Finance career cluster and OSPI Business & Marketing program area. Delivers occupational/technical competencies in double-entry accounting, the accounting cycle, and financial statement preparation - core preparatory CTE content tied to industry standards (AICPA/NAF) and linked CTSOs (FBLA, DECA, BPA). Standard articulated HS CTE course at WA community colleges.

Accounting II aligns directly with Finance cluster and Business & Marketing OSPI program area. Delivers occupation-specific technical competencies (partnership/corporate accounting, investments, income distribution) that map to recognized postsecondary credentials (CPA pathway, bookkeeping certifications) and FBLA/DECA CTSO integration. Preparatory-level course in a clear career pathway sequence with industry-defined standards (GAAP). Strong CTE fit.

Managerial/cost accounting aligns to the Finance and Business/Management career clusters under OSPI's Business & Marketing program area. Course delivers occupational competencies (job order/process costing, standard costs, budgets, internal controls) used in real-world manufacturing/business contexts, supporting preparatory pathway toward accounting/finance credentials. Appropriate CTE classification.

BUS&201 Business Law aligns with the Business Management & Administration career cluster and OSPI's Business & Marketing program area. Content (UCC, contracts, sales, commercial paper, agency, business organizations, employment law) maps to industry-relevant legal competencies foundational to business pathways and supports FBLA/DECA CTSO event areas (Business Law, Entrepreneurship). The legal-institutions/legal-reasoning component also reasonably supports Social Studies elective credit (law/government content). Keep both classifications.

Project Management aligns with the Business/Management Career Cluster and OSPI's Business & Marketing program area. Course delivers occupational/technical competencies (project planning, risk management, procurement, stakeholder communication) and industry-standard tooling (Microsoft Project) consistent with PMI-aligned skills. Applied, performance-based work (develop and manage project plans) supports CTE performance assessment expectations and employability skills integration. Reasonable Preparatory designation for business pathways; supports progression toward recognized credentials (e.g., CAPM/PMP foundations).

CMST&220 Public Speaking is a general-education communication studies transfer course. While the description mentions "business realities," it is taught as a general-ed speech course without alignment to any of the 16 national Career Clusters, no industry/nationally-defined technical standards (e.g., no Arts/AV/Communications production credential), no documented WA CTE Curriculum Framework, no CTSO linkage, no advisory-committee skills-gap justification, and no Exploratory/Preparatory CIP designation. The CTE rubric explicitly flags CMST&220 as a course that sounds CTE-adjacent but is not aligned with K-12 CTE standards. It does fit ELA credit (oral communication is part of ELA/Speaking & Listening standards).

Early Childhood Education course aligns with Education & Training career cluster and FCS program area. Develops occupational skills (outdoor classroom design, nature-based curriculum, sustainability practices) applied in real-world early childhood settings. ECE coursework at WA community colleges is a recognized CTE pathway leading to credentials like CDA and state ECE certificates, with Educators Rising CTSO linkage. Content is applied/practical rather than academic transfer.

Early Childhood Education practicum aligns with Education & Training career cluster and FCS/Human Services program areas. Course involves applied practicum work (curriculum development, lesson planning, classroom environment design) which delivers occupational competencies for child care/early learning pathway, consistent with preparatory CTE framing.

Early Childhood Education practicum with infant/toddler specialization aligns to Human Services cluster (Early Childhood Development pathway) and OSPI's FCS/Education & Training program area. Practical field experience plus seminar delivers occupational competencies, employability skills in real-world child care settings, and progresses toward recognized credentials (CDA, state ECE certificates). Clear preparatory CTE course.

Early Childhood Education practicum aligns with the Education & Training career cluster and OSPI's Family & Consumer Sciences/Human Services program areas. Responsive caregiving, lesson planning, family collaboration, and developmental theory are occupation-specific competencies tied to ECE credentials (Washington State Stackable ECE Certificates, CDA). The practicum format delivers performance-based skill demonstration in real-world settings, matching CTE preparatory course requirements.

Early Childhood Education courses align directly with the Education & Training career cluster (one of 16 national clusters) and OSPI's Family & Consumer Sciences program area. ECE230 specifically addresses occupational competencies for ECE professionals working with special needs children — inclusion practices, positive guidance techniques, and classroom strategies are industry-specific technical skills tied to ECE credentialing pathways (e.g., Washington STARS training, CDA credential, AA-T in ECE). The course supports the ECE preparatory pathway leading to recognized postsecondary credentials and workforce entry. Content is applied/practical (strategies, techniques) rather than purely academic, fitting the CTE performance-assessment model. Aligns with Educators Rising CTSO and addresses real workforce need in early learning.

ECE231 is a supervised practicum in Early Childhood Education with a school-age specialization, including a seminar. It aligns to the Education & Training career cluster and OSPI's Family & Consumer Sciences program area. The field experience delivers preparatory, occupation-specific technical competencies (child development application, age-appropriate care/curriculum, professional practice) assessed via performance in a real industry setting rather than academic exams. It supports progression toward recognized ECE credentials (Washington STARS, CDA, state ECE certificates) and pairs with FCCLA as the aligned CTSO. This is a textbook CTE preparatory practicum.

Early Childhood Education course aligns with Human Services career cluster and OSPI Family & Consumer Sciences program area. Anti-bias curriculum development is occupation-specific preparatory content for ECE workforce, addressing industry standards (NAEYC) and applied skills for working with children/families. Fits CTE strands.

ECE286 is an Early Childhood Education practicum with a Leadership specialization, including supervised field experience and a seminar. ECE aligns to the Education & Training career cluster (OSPI FCS/Education pathway) with documented industry standards (NAEYC, WA State Core Competencies for Early Care and Education professionals), occupation-specific competencies, and a clear progression toward the Washington State ECE Initial/Short/State Certificate stackable credentials. The practicum format inherently delivers performance-based assessment in a real-world business/industry context (licensed childcare/preschool setting) and the leadership specialization directly addresses CTE Core Leadership and Employability Skills. This is a preparatory CTE course, not a general academic transfer course.

ECE287 is a supervised practicum in Child Development with seminar, aligning to the Education & Training career cluster and OSPI's FCS program area (Early Childhood Education pathway). It delivers preparatory, occupation-specific competencies through real-world field placement (a performance assessment by design), maps to NAEYC/WA Early Learning Guidelines industry standards, and progresses toward recognized ECE credentials (CDA, State Initial Certificate, AAS-ECE). The practicum format inherently embeds employability and leadership skills in an industry setting, satisfying core CTE requirements. Not a general-academic transfer course.

Early Childhood Education practicum with Family Child Care specialization aligns to Human Services career cluster (Early Childhood Development & Services pathway) and OSPI's Family & Consumer Sciences program area. Practicum with field experience, seminar, and observations delivers occupation-specific preparatory competencies, performance-based assessment, and employability skills tied to a recognized postsecondary credential pathway (CDA, state child care licensing). FCCLA is the aligned CTSO. Strong CTE fit.

ECE292 is a foundational course in Clover Park's Early Childhood Education program, which aligns to the Education & Training career cluster (and FCS/Education program area in WA). ECE in Washington has a clearly defined CTE pathway with stackable credentials (CDA, ECE Initial/Short/State Certificates) tied to state childcare workforce licensing requirements — distinguishing it from generic 'Intro to Education' transfer courses. The course supports occupational competencies (applying child development theory to teaching practice), connects to FCCLA as CTSO, and feeds a preparatory sequence toward recognized credentials. Keep as CTE.

ECE297 is a practicum in Early Childhood Education with a Special Needs specialization. ECE aligns directly with the Education & Training career cluster (CIP 19.0709) and OSPI's Family & Consumer Sciences program area. The practicum format provides supervised real-world field experience with a scheduled seminar, satisfying performance-assessment and applied-skills requirements. This is a preparatory CTE course advancing students toward ECE credentials (Washington State ECE Initial/Short Certificate stackable credentials), making it a clear fit for CTE.

ECE290CAP is a capstone portfolio course within an Early Care and Education degree program, directly aligned with the Education & Training career cluster (one of the 16 national clusters) and OSPI's program area. The course explicitly addresses key CTE strands: (1) self-marketing goals tied to workforce entry in the ECE field, (2) portfolio production demonstrating best practice/competencies (performance assessment, not exams), (3) a formal portfolio review process mirroring industry credentialing practices, and (4) preparation for postsecondary credentials/employment in early childhood education. This is a Preparatory CTE course tied to the ECE pathway and CDA/state ECE certification progression. Employability skills (self-marketing, professional portfolio, feedback integration) are central to the course design. Keep as CTE.

Aligns with Education & Training career cluster and FCS/Human Services program areas. STARS 30-hour basics meets WA state licensing requirements (industry-recognized credential via MERIT), addresses occupation-specific competencies (child development, guidance, health/safety/nutrition, professional practice), and supports a preparatory early childhood education pathway leading to a recognized credential. Strong CTE fit.

Intro to Early Childhood Education aligns with the Education & Training career cluster and OSPI's Family & Consumer Sciences program area. ECE coursework is a recognized CTE preparatory pathway leading to industry credentials (CDA, State ECE certificates/stackables) and connects to FCCLA/Educators Rising CTSOs. Content covers program models, best practices, and observation of children/professionals — applied competencies tied to workforce in childcare/preschool settings, not pure academic transfer.

ECED107 aligns directly with Education & Training / Human Services career clusters under OSPI's FCS program area. Delivers occupation-specific competencies (mandated reporter, child health/safety/nutrition protocols) tied to Washington State Early Learning Guidelines, NAEYC standards, and MERIT/STARS childcare credentialing. Stacks toward ECE certificates/AAS and childcare workforce credentials with FCCLA CTSO linkage. Preparatory CTE, not general academic content.

ECED120 is an Early Childhood Education practicum at Clover Park Technical College, aligning directly with the Human Services cluster (Early Childhood Development pathway) and Education & Training cluster. The practicum format delivers performance-based assessment in real-world childcare settings, addresses industry-defined competencies (child health/safety, development, culturally responsive practice tied to NAEYC and WA State ECE Core Competencies), and is part of WA's stackable ECE credential pathway (Initial/Short/State Certificates, AAS) linked to MERIT/STARS workforce registry. This is a Preparatory CTE course with clear occupational focus and recognized postsecondary credential progression.

Early Childhood Education aligns with the Education & Training career cluster and OSPI's Family & Consumer Sciences program area. Infant/toddler care is a recognized CTE preparatory pathway leading to credentials like CDA (Child Development Associate) and state ECE certifications. Content addresses industry-defined competencies (developmentally appropriate practices, caregiver role, family partnerships) consistent with NAEYC standards used in WA CTE frameworks. FCCLA is the aligned CTSO. Course is appropriately classified as CTE.

ECED&134 Family Child Care aligns directly with multiple CTE strands. It fits the Human Services career cluster (Early Childhood Development & Services pathway) and OSPI's Family & Consumer Sciences program area. Content covers occupation-specific competencies: licensing requirements (industry/state regulatory standards), business management (entrepreneurship/employability skills applied in real-world context), health/safety/nutrition (industry-defined early childhood standards aligned with NAEYC and WA DCYF licensing), behavior guidance, and child growth/development. This is clearly Preparatory CTE leading toward recognized credentials (State of WA family home childcare licensing, CDA pathway, STARS training credit). FCCLA is the aligned CTSO. Course is occupational/technical, not general academic content.

Aligns with Education & Training career cluster and FCS/Human Services program area. Covers occupation-specific competencies: program administration, WA State licensing compliance, NAEYC standards - all industry-defined standards for the early childhood education pathway. Preparatory course supporting progression toward ECE credentials (CDA, NAEYC). Applied/real-world framing (operate, manage, assess programs) rather than academic theory.

ECED160 Curriculum Development is an Early Childhood Education course aligning with the Education & Training career cluster and OSPI's Family & Consumer Sciences program area. It addresses occupation-specific competencies (learning theory, program planning, curriculum design across developmental domains) that progress toward recognized ECE credentials (Washington State ECE Initial/Short Certificate, CDA). This is Preparatory CTE content tied to the early childhood education pathway, not general academic coursework.

ECED&170 aligns with Human Services / Education & Training career clusters and falls under OSPI's Family & Consumer Sciences program area. Early Childhood Education is a recognized CTE preparatory pathway with industry standards (NAEYC), leadership/employability skills, applied performance assessments (designing/evaluating learning environments), and progression toward recognized credentials (Initial/Short Certificate, CDA, AAS). Course content focuses on occupational competencies for childcare/preschool professionals, not general academic content.

Early Childhood Education course at a technical college aligned to the Education & Training career cluster and OSPI's FCS program area. Delivers occupational competencies in applied teaching strategies for language/literacy development (birth-age 8), part of Clover Park's ECE workforce pathway leading to state-recognized ECE certificates and CDA credentials. Preparatory CTE, not general-ed academic transfer.

Early Childhood Education observation/assessment course aligns with OSPI CTE program area Family & Consumer Sciences and the Education & Training career cluster (Early Childhood Development & Services pathway). Preparatory course delivering occupation-specific technical competencies (child observation, assessment documentation, family communication) tied to ECE workforce credentials (CDA, state ECE certifications) and the AAS-ECE pathway at WA community colleges.

ECON& 201 is an academic-transfer microeconomics course covering pure economic theory (scarcity, supply/demand, market structures, comparative advantage, income distribution). The guidance explicitly lists ECON& 201/202 as NOT CTE absent Finance or Business/Management pathway framing and industry standards. Description shows no career cluster alignment, no industry-defined technical standards (e.g., NAF), no Leadership/Employability Skills integration, no performance assessments (labs, technical skill demonstrations), no CTSO linkage, and no CIP-coded Exploratory/Preparatory designation. Course is a general-education social science transfer course that satisfies Social Studies - Elective credit only.

Pure academic-transfer macroeconomics covering GDP, fiscal/monetary policy, and theory. No career cluster alignment, no industry standards (NAF Finance not referenced), no Curriculum Framework, no leadership/employability skills, no performance assessments, no CTSO/advisory committee link. OSPI guidance explicitly excludes ECON& 201/202 absent Finance or Business/Management pathway framing. Fits Social Studies - Elective as economics content.

Nursing Assistant Theory aligns directly with the Health Science career cluster and OSPI Health Sciences program area. It prepares students for the Washington State Nursing Assistant Certified (NAC) licensure exam, a recognized postsecondary credential, satisfying industry-standard alignment, occupational/technical competencies, and preparatory pathway designation. This is a classic Skilled & Technical Sciences / Health Sciences CTE course.

Nursing Assistant Certified (NAC) coursework aligns directly with the Health Science career cluster and OSPI's Health Sciences program area. Clinical hours and a final skills exam constitute performance assessments leading to a recognized postsecondary credential (WA State Nursing Assistant Certification), satisfying preparatory CTE requirements.

Nursing Assistant Certified (NAC) clinical experience in long-term care directly aligns with Health Science career cluster and Skilled & Technical Sciences OSPI program area. Course delivers supervised clinical skill demonstrations (performance assessments), leads to a recognized postsecondary credential (WA State Nursing Assistant Certification via DOH), and represents Preparatory occupation-specific training. Course is part of the Early Childhood Education BAS program, aligning to the Education & Training career cluster (FCS program area). As a capstone reflective seminar within a CTE-aligned BAS degree sequence with prerequisites in the ECED pathway, it supports occupational/preparatory CTE framing toward early childhood credentials.

ECED450 is the capstone for Green River's Early Childhood Education program, which aligns to the OSPI CTE Education & Training career cluster (early learning workforce pathway). The course requires synthesis of program competencies, a culminating project presented to faculty, and demonstration of how research informs professional work in early learning - meeting the performance-assessment and real-world application requirements of CTE. ECE programs link to recognized credentials (CDA, WA STARS, AA in Early Care & Education), satisfying the postsecondary credential progression strand. Capstone format inherently delivers technical skill demonstration rather than solely exams/essays. Appropriate CTE classification.

ECON& 201 Micro Economics is an academic-transfer economics course covering microeconomic theory (scarcity, choice, demand/supply, competition, cost). The audit guidance explicitly flags "Economics academic-transfer (ECON& 201/202) absent Finance or Business/Management pathway framing and industry standards" as NOT aligned with K-12 CTE. The description shows no CTE markers: no Career Cluster alignment, no industry/nationally-defined standards (e.g., NAF for Finance), no Washington CTE Program Standards crosswalk, no Leadership/Employability Skills integration, no performance assessments (labs/projects/technical demos), no CTSO linkage, no Exploratory/Preparatory designation, and no progression toward postsecondary credential or certification. The "Satisfies Requirement: Social Science" tag and theory-focused outcomes confirm it functions as a Social Science transfer course, appropriately mapped to Social Studies - Elective.

ECON&202 Macroeconomics is an academic-transfer Social Science course covering GDP, national income, employment, and price levels via the Production Possibilities Model. It has no Career Cluster alignment, no industry/nationally-defined technical standards (e.g., NAF Finance), no documented WA CTE Curriculum Framework, no leadership/employability skills integration, no performance assessments (labs/technical demos), and no CTSO linkage. Per the guidance, ECON& 201/202 absent Finance or Business/Management pathway framing and industry standards do not qualify as CTE. It fits Social Studies - Elective only.

Career exploration/college survival course focused on transfer pathways and program-of-study planning, not occupational/technical competencies. No industry standards (ABET, NCEES), no documented WA CTE Curriculum Framework, no performance-based technical skill demonstrations, no CTSO (TSA/SkillsUSA) integration, and no advisory-committee skills-gap link. Closely parallels "college success/seminar" exclusions. Mentions of programming/engineering design are introductory exposure for transfer planning, not preparatory STEM cluster competencies. Best fits a general Elective.

ENGR&104 Introduction to Design aligns with the STEM career cluster and Skilled & Technical Sciences/STEM OSPI program area. The course explicitly teaches the engineering design process, teamwork, professionalism and ethics, quantitative analysis, and real-world open-ended problems — directly mapping to CTE Core Employability Skills and 21st Century Skills integration. Performance assessments include design projects, journals, and oral presentations rather than solely exams, satisfying the CTE performance-assessment requirement. As an introductory engineering course, it functions as Exploratory within an engineering pathway leading toward recognized postsecondary credentials. While it is also a transferable academic engineering course, its applied design-process focus and employability-skill integration justify the CTE classification.

ENGR 106 is an academic-transfer engineering problem-solving course covering dimensional analysis, vector algebra, Newton's laws, statistics, and error analysis. The course explicitly states it "Satisfies Requirement: Natural Science," is gated by MATH& 142 (precalculus) and ENGR 100, and focuses on theoretical engineering computation rather than occupation-specific technical competencies. There is no documented Curriculum Framework tying it to WA State CTE Program Standards, no Leadership/Employability skills integration, no CTSO (e.g., SkillsUSA/TSA) linkage, no advisory-committee/skills-gap justification, and no progression toward an industry credential or apprenticeship. While STEM is a Career Cluster, OSPI requires CTE-approved courses to deliver applied technical/occupational competencies plus leadership/employability components — this is a pre-engineering transfer course, not an OSPI-approved CTE course. It fits Science (Non-Lab) credit because the description shows no required lab component (only problem-solving, statistics, and error analysis).

Engineering Graphics is a lab-based CAD/drafting course teaching parametric 3-D modeling, technical sketching, visualization, and graphic communication — core competencies aligned to the STEM cluster (Engineering & Technology pathway) and the Skilled & Technical Sciences program area. Content maps to industry drafting/CAD standards (ANSI/ASME Y14) and supports progression toward engineering/drafting credentials. Performance assessments are lab/project-based (3-D models, drawings), not exam-only. Fits CTE classification.

Engineering Materials aligns with the STEM career cluster (Engineering & Technology pathway) and addresses applied engineering competencies used by practicing engineers — consistent with CTE STEM program area. It also satisfies a Natural Science requirement and covers chemistry-based content (atomic bonding, crystalline structure) with CHEM& 161 prerequisite, indicating lab science rigor. Best classified as both CTE (STEM cluster) and Science (Lab).

ENGR&204 Electrical Circuits is a pre-engineering transfer course with strong alignment to the STEM career cluster (engineering pathway) and Skilled & Technical Sciences program area, covering industry-relevant technical competencies (circuit analysis, Thevenin/Norton, nodal/mesh analysis) foundational to electrical engineering and electronics technician careers. It also satisfies a Natural Science requirement, has a lab fee ($2.00), and applies physics/math (PHYS& 222 and MATH& 163 prerequisites) to applied technical problem-solving — supporting Science (Lab) equivalency. While the description does not explicitly document Leadership/Employability skills or CTSO integration typical of K-12 CTE Curriculum Frameworks, the applied technical/engineering nature and progression toward recognized engineering/technical credentials supports keeping CTE while adding Lab Science as a cross-walked equivalency.

ENGR&214 Statics is a foundational engineering mechanics course that aligns with the STEM career cluster (Engineering & Technology pathway) under OSPI's STEM program area. It addresses engineering design process, structural component analysis, and applied problem-solving in real-world engineering contexts — core preparatory CTE content for engineering pathways. The course explicitly satisfies a Natural Science requirement at Green River, indicating it also qualifies as science credit. Since the description mentions vector notation/calculus and mathematical modeling but no hands-on lab component (it's analytical/theoretical mechanics, not a wet/measurement lab), Science (Non-Lab) is the appropriate science designation. CTE classification is defensible for STEM-cluster preparatory engineering coursework, and the dual science credit reflects its documented Natural Science alignment.

ENGR& 215 Dynamics is a pure academic transfer engineering course teaching particle and rigid body mechanics using vector analysis (kinematics, kinetics, momentum, energy). It is a prerequisite-heavy theoretical course (requires ENGR& 214, MATH& 152, PHYS& 221) explicitly designated by the institution as satisfying a Natural Science requirement. While engineering relates to the STEM career cluster broadly, this course lacks the OSPI CTE requirements: no industry/nationally-defined technical standards (no NICET, no engineering technician credential alignment), no documented WA CTE Curriculum Framework, no Leadership/Employability Skills integration, no CTSO linkage (TSA/SkillsUSA), no performance/lab assessments tied to industry competencies, no Exploratory/Preparatory CIP designation, and no progression toward a recognized technical credential or apprenticeship. It is an academic-transfer engineering science course (akin to MATH&/PHYS& transfer courses called out as non-CTE in the rules). Best fits Science (Non-Lab) credit since the institution itself classifies it as Natural Science but the description shows no lab component — it is theoretical mechanics with vector analysis problem-solving.

Calculus-based engineering science transfer course (prereqs CHEM& 161, MATH& 152, PHYS& 221) that Green River explicitly designates as satisfying Natural Science. Outcomes are theoretical analysis of thermodynamic laws/cycles, with no Curriculum Framework, no industry certification alignment (NCCER/NATEF/etc.), no Leadership/Employability Skills integration, no CTSO linkage, and no Exploratory/Preparatory CIP designation. Falls under the "pure academic science transfer without applied CTE framing" exclusion. Best fits Science (Lab) credit per the college's own Natural Science designation.

ENGR250 aligns with the STEM career cluster (Engineering & Technology pathway) and the OSPI STEM program area. It teaches applied numerical methods using MATLAB to solve real engineering problems (mechanics, thermodynamics, electrical circuits), which delivers occupational/technical competencies relevant to engineering practice and progression toward postsecondary engineering credentials. The applied, project/problem-based nature supports performance assessments via computational implementations rather than solely exams. It also delivers substantive physical-science/engineering-science content (mechanics, thermodynamics, circuits) supporting a Science (Non-Lab) credit since the course is computational/simulation-based rather than wet-lab. Prerequisite MATH& 254 (differential equations) indicates rigorous STEM content. Conservatively retaining CTE while adding Science (Non-Lab) as a cross-walk option.

ENGR271 Digital Circuits and Systems aligns with the STEM career cluster (Engineering & Technology pathway) and fits OSPI's STEM/Skilled & Technical Sciences program areas. Content covers industry-relevant technical competencies (digital logic design, Boolean algebra, FSMs, combinational/sequential circuits, programmable logic devices, ALU/memory/I/O) using CAD tools — directly applicable to electronics/computer engineering technician pathways. Weekly labs satisfy performance-assessment requirement (technical skill demonstrations, not just exams). The course also explicitly "Satisfies Requirement: Natural Science" at Green River and includes weekly laboratories with measurable lab outcomes, supporting Science (Lab) credit as well. Keeping CTE and adding Science (Lab) reflects the dual-credit nature of applied engineering coursework with a documented lab component.

Introduction to Information Technology aligns with the IT national Career Cluster under the Skilled & Technical Sciences/STEM program area. Outcomes cover hardware, software, OS/application functionality, security, networking, and web development — foundational IT competencies that map to industry certification pathways (CompTIA IT Fundamentals/A+, Network+, Security+). Functions as an Exploratory IT course providing broad industry exposure. Reasonable to keep as CTE.

Scripting for Cybersecurity aligns squarely with the IT national career cluster and OSPI Skilled & Technical Sciences program area. Course outcomes emphasize applied technical competencies (scripting, flow control, libraries, version control, APIs) with a cybersecurity focus, supporting progression toward industry credentials (CompTIA Security+, Network+, Linux+). Hands-on script creation provides performance-based assessment rather than purely academic exams, fitting CTE preparatory programming.

Aligns to IT Career Cluster with cybersecurity, hardware, OS, networking — maps to industry certs (CompTIA A+/Network+/Security+). Includes install/configure/troubleshoot performance competencies, fitting Skilled & Technical Sciences program area. Clear preparatory IT pathway content, not generic intro to computers.

IT cluster networking course (IPv4/IPv6, subnetting) aligns to industry certs (CompTIA Network+, Cisco CCNA) with technical/performance skill demonstrations. Fits Skilled & Technical Sciences / IT cluster.
Aligns to IT cluster, Cisco CCNA industry-recognized credential, hands-on router/switch configuration (performance skills), Skilled & Technical Sciences program area.

Aligns to IT cluster (Skilled & Technical Sciences) with direct industry certification prep (CompTIA Security+), occupational technical competencies, and clear postsecondary credential pathway — textbook CTE.

Windows Server Administration aligns directly with the IT career cluster (Network Systems pathway). Course delivers occupation-specific technical competencies (Active Directory, Group Policy, DNS, DHCP, PowerShell, virtualization) that map to industry certifications like CompTIA Server+, Microsoft MCSA/MD-102, and Network+. Preparatory CTE course with hands-on configuration/management skills rather than general academic content.

IT Work Experience aligns directly with the Information Technology career cluster. The course is a supervised work-based learning experience tied to an IT program (install/configure/support/troubleshoot operating systems referenced in program outcomes), which embodies the Preparatory CTE model: applied technical competencies, real-world industry context, and integrated employability skills (working with others in the field, adjusting to a career, professional settings). Work-based learning is an OSPI-recognized CTE delivery method and a hallmark of skilled & technical sciences pathways. Pass/no credit and instructor consent are typical for CTE cooperative work experience. Keep as CTE.

Aligns to IT career cluster (Skilled & Technical Sciences program area). Covers Linux admin, file/directory permissions, OS security, shell scripting - occupational technical competencies tied to industry standards (CompTIA Linux+/Security+ pathway). Hands-on Terminal-based performance tasks satisfy CTE performance assessment requirement. Clear preparatory IT pathway course.

Database Fundamentals aligns directly with the IT Career Cluster and OSPI Skilled & Technical Sciences program area. Course delivers occupation-specific technical competencies (DBMS management, SQL DDL/DML, constraint design) tied to industry-recognized database administration skills (Oracle/Microsoft SQL, CompTIA Data+). Hands-on real-world troubleshooting exercises satisfy the performance-assessment requirement (labs/projects, not solely exams). This is preparatory CTE content distinct from general 'Intro to Computers' — it maps to a defined IT/database pathway with clear postsecondary credential progression. Meets the core CTE strands for cluster alignment, technical standards, and applied performance assessment.

Routing and Switching Essentials aligns directly with the IT Career Cluster (Network Systems pathway). Content mirrors Cisco CCNA industry certification standards (device CLI configuration, IPv4/IPv6 subnetting, VLANs, NAT, ACLs, DHCP, routing protocols, WAN), which is a recognized postsecondary credential pathway. Course is preparatory with hands-on technical skill demonstrations (lab-based configuration), satisfying performance assessment requirements. Fits Skilled & Technical Sciences program area. Clear CTE classification.

Cisco CCST Cybersecurity prep aligns directly with IT career cluster, industry certification (Cisco CCST), hands-on labs, and clear occupational/technical competencies in security principles, network security, vulnerability assessment, and incident handling. Fits Skilled & Technical Sciences / IT cluster as Preparatory CTE with recognized postsecondary credential pathway.

Windows Automation and Scripting (PowerShell) aligns directly with the IT career cluster, specifically the Network Systems/Information Support & Services pathways. It delivers occupation-specific technical competencies (scripting, automation, system administration) used in real-world IT operations roles, supports industry certifications (Microsoft MCSA/Azure Admin, CompTIA), and includes performance-based assessment via writing, testing, and executing scripts (not just exams). Prerequisite IT 160 indicates a preparatory sequence within an IT pathway. Fits OSPI's Skilled & Technical Sciences / STEM program area. Clearly CTE-appropriate.

Aligns with IT Career Cluster (Skilled & Technical Sciences program area). Prepares students for CompTIA Cloud+ industry certification — an explicitly recognized IT cluster credential. Covers technical competencies (virtualization, infrastructure, security, resource management) with hands-on systems management. Clear preparatory designation toward recognized postsecondary/industry credential.

IT245 Identity and Access Management aligns directly with the Information Technology career cluster under OSPI's Skilled & Technical Sciences program area. Course outcomes (configure/maintain directory services, apply user/resource policies, use certificates for authN/authZ, plan access management) are occupation-specific technical competencies that map to industry-recognized IT credentials (CompTIA Security+, Microsoft Identity/AD certifications). The IT 160 prerequisite indicates a preparatory sequence within a defined pathway, and the lab/configuration nature supports performance-based assessment rather than purely academic exams. Fits CTE classification.
CompTIA Project+ prep aligns to Business/Management and IT clusters with industry-recognized CompTIA certification, employability skills, and applied project management competencies — clear CTE fit.

Advanced IT networking course (OSPF, VLAN routing, SDN, VPNs) with hands-on configuration/troubleshooting labs aligns directly with the IT career cluster and Skilled & Technical Sciences program area. Maps to industry-recognized networking certifications (Cisco CCNA/CCNP), satisfying CTE technical competency and performance assessment requirements.

IT cluster course covering access control, cryptography, risk management, BCP/DR — aligns to IT cluster industry standards (CompTIA Security+, CISSP domains). Preparatory occupational competencies within a BAS IT pathway.

Network Security and Firewalls aligns with the IT career cluster under Skilled & Technical Sciences. It explicitly prepares students for entry-level network security specialist careers (preparatory designation), uses hands-on labs and troubleshooting (performance assessments), and maps to industry-recognized credentials (CompTIA Security+, Cisco CCNA Security). Clear CTE fit.

Aligns directly with IT national career cluster and Skilled & Technical Sciences program area. Occupation-specific (Security Analyst/SOC) preparatory course with industry-aligned technical competencies (monitoring, detection, forensics, incident response) that map to recognized cybersecurity credentials (CompTIA Security+/CySA+, NIST frameworks). Embedded in BAS IT pathway supporting postsecondary credential progression and high-demand workforce need. Performance-based skill demonstrations inherent to forensics/SOC work satisfy CTE assessment requirements.

IT cluster cybersecurity course teaching Kibana configuration for network security monitoring - clearly aligned to IT cluster, occupation-specific (Preparatory), part of BAS Cybersecurity pathway, with hands-on technical skill demonstrations (configure visualizations, dashboards, filters). Industry-standard tooling (Elastic/Kibana) common in SOC/SIEM roles.

Aligns to IT cluster (Skilled & Technical Sciences); prepares for Microsoft Azure Infrastructure Solutions certification — recognized industry credential. Outcomes are occupational/technical (Azure App Service, VMs, storage, AD, virtual networks) with performance-based competencies, fitting Preparatory CTE.

IT 385 Advanced Scripting and Automation aligns squarely with the Information Technology career cluster under OSPI's Skilled & Technical Sciences / STEM program area. It teaches occupation-specific technical competencies (Ansible, Python, PowerShell, YAML) directly tied to industry-recognized DevOps/SysAdmin roles and certifications (Red Hat Ansible, CompTIA Linux+, Microsoft scripting). The course is preparatory (embedded in a BAS in IT program with IT 102 and IT 224 prerequisites), delivers hands-on performance-based skill demonstrations through script development, and maps to real-world IT automation workforce demand. This is not a generic "intro to computers" course — it's advanced, applied, certification-relevant technical training that fits CTE IT cluster standards.

IT 450 Cybersecurity Analysis aligns directly with the IT career cluster (Information Support & Services / Network Systems pathway). Course delivers occupation-specific technical competencies: signature/reputation/behavior-based detection methods, network traffic monitoring, incident analysis, ticket creation/resolution — all hands-on industry-standard SOC analyst tasks. Part of a BAS Cybersecurity & Networking program tied to recognized industry certifications (CompTIA Security+, CySA+, Cisco). Performance assessments are lab/project-based (weekly traffic monitoring, ticket workflows) rather than purely academic. Preparatory CTE course with clear progression toward postsecondary credential and workforce skills-gap (cybersecurity workforce shortage). Fits OSPI Skilled & Technical Sciences / STEM program areas.

IT460 Threat Analysis aligns directly with the IT Career Cluster (Cybersecurity pathway). It delivers occupation-specific technical competencies (ethical hacking, intrusion detection, vulnerability auditing, network security across platforms) tied to industry-recognized standards (CEH/CompTIA Security+ aligned content). The course is preparatory, embedded in the BAS in IT program (progression toward postsecondary credential), and emphasizes applied technical skill demonstrations rather than purely academic exams. This fits OSPI's Skilled & Technical Sciences / STEM program area framing for IT cybersecurity.

Aligns with IT career cluster, Skilled & Technical Sciences program area; preps for AWS SysOps Administrator industry certification with applied technical competencies (deploy, manage, operate cloud systems).

IT cluster course aligned to industry certification (CCSP). Covers cloud security design, data security, infrastructure/application security, and compliance — occupational technical competencies with clear postsecondary credential pathway. Fits IT cluster / Skilled & Technical Sciences program area.

Capstone in cybersecurity/networking aligns squarely with IT career cluster and Skilled & Technical Sciences program area. Team-based project to plan, implement, secure, and document a network for a real/simulated company delivers performance-based technical skill demonstration, embedded employability skills (stakeholder management, WBS, status reporting, teamwork), and preparatory occupation-specific competencies linked to industry credentials (CompTIA/Cisco). Strong fit for HS CTE credit.

Independent Study with no fixed curriculum, no documented CTE Curriculum Framework, no industry standards alignment, no embedded leadership/employability skills, and no performance assessments tied to a Career Cluster. Content is determined ad hoc between student and instructor and can be in any subject, so it cannot be approved as an OSPI CTE course. Best fits a general Elective.

Principles of Accounting I aligns with the Finance and Business/Management career clusters under OSPI's Business & Marketing program area. Content (accounting cycle, merchandising operations, receivables, inventories) delivers occupation-specific technical competencies tied to industry standards (GAAP) and supports recognized postsecondary/professional credential pathways (CPA, bookkeeping certifications). Commonly cross-walked to CTE in WA dual-credit agreements; CTSO alignment available via FBLA/DECA. Keep as CTE.

Principles of Accounting II aligns with the Business/Management and Finance career clusters under OSPI's Business & Marketing program area. It covers occupation-specific technical competencies (fixed assets, liabilities, partnerships, corporations, statement of cash flows) that map to industry accounting standards and feed directly into recognized postsecondary pathways and credentials (CPA pipeline, bookkeeping/accounting certifications). This is the kind of applied business/finance content OSPI recognizes for CTE credit, comparable to FBLA/DECA-aligned accounting pathway courses. Keep as CTE.

Managerial accounting aligns to the Finance and Business/Management career clusters under OSPI's Business & Marketing program area. Course delivers occupation-specific technical competencies (cost analysis, budgeting, managerial decision-making) consistent with industry-recognized accounting standards and supports progression toward postsecondary credentials (CPA pathway, bookkeeping certifications). Fits Preparatory CTE designation with clear workforce-need justification.

BUS195 is a Business-prefixed independent study where topic, scope, and learning objectives are negotiated between student and instructor. Because it sits under the BUS subject, the default expectation is alignment with the Business/Management or Marketing career cluster (OSPI Business & Marketing program area). An independent study can incorporate industry standards, employability skills, and performance-based deliverables when structured by the supervising business instructor. While the open-ended nature is marginal (no fixed curriculum framework), the BUS prefix grounds it in a CTE cluster, so conservatively leaning keep_target is appropriate.

Business Statistics applies statistical methods to economics/business contexts, aligning with the Business/Management and Finance career clusters under OSPI's Business & Marketing program area. It delivers applied quantitative competencies (descriptive/inferential statistics, regression, hypothesis testing) used in industry decision-making, supporting CTE classification. However, the course also delivers rigorous college-level mathematics content (probability, distributions, confidence intervals, hypothesis testing) with a MATH 099 prerequisite, satisfying HS Math credit equivalency. Recommend adding Math alongside CTE.

Business Finance aligns directly with the Finance career cluster (one of 16 national clusters) and OSPI's Business & Marketing program area. Content covers industry-standard finance competencies (time value of money, risk/return, securities valuation, capital budgeting, financial statement analysis) consistent with NAF Finance pathway standards. The applied focus on financial management decision-making, goal setting, and budgeting demonstrates occupational/technical competencies rather than pure academic theory, fitting preparatory CTE coursework toward finance careers.

BUS&101 Intro to Business covers management, marketing, finance, and HR within global/economic/ethical business environments. This directly aligns with the Business Management & Administration, Marketing, and Finance career clusters under OSPI's Business & Marketing program area. It functions as an exploratory CTE course providing broad industry exposure across multiple business pathways, consistent with K-12 CTE Intro to Business framing (often paired with FBLA/DECA CTSOs).

BUS&201 Business Law aligns with the Business/Management and Finance career clusters under OSPI's Business & Marketing program area. The course content (business contracts, torts, legal system as applied to business operations) supports occupational competencies relevant to business pathways and is commonly cross-walked to FBLA/DECA CTSO competitive events (Business Law, Entrepreneurship). It is a standard component of preparatory business pathway sequences and connects to recognized postsecondary business credentials. The legal system content also reasonably supports Social Studies - Elective credit (civics/law content). Conservative keep with both classifications retained.

CMST& 220 Public Speaking is listed as Humanities and focuses on general academic speech communication—preparing/presenting informative and persuasive speeches using logic and reasoning. The description shows no alignment to any of the 16 career clusters, no industry/national technical standards (e.g., no Arts/AV/Communications media-production competencies, no Business/Marketing pathway scaffolding), no Washington State CTE Program Standards reference, no Leadership/Employability Skills framework, no CTSO integration (DECA/FBLA/SkillsUSA), no advisory-committee skills-gap justification, and no progression toward an industry credential. The OSPI guidance and "sounds like CTE but is NOT" list explicitly flag CMST& 220 as general-ed speech that doesn't qualify as CTE absent business/marketing or media-production cluster alignment. The communication-focused content (oral composition, rhetoric, argumentation) does support ELA credit, which is a recognized core-subject equivalency for speech courses.

Course aligns with IT career cluster - teaches practical programming skills (algorithm development, debugging, requirements verification) which are occupation-specific technical competencies. Computer ethics component addresses employability/21st century skills in real-world IT contexts. Fits Skilled & Technical Sciences / STEM program area with clear pathway toward IT industry credentials.

CS143 Computer Science II Java aligns with the IT Career Cluster (Programming & Software Development pathway) and STEM program area. Object-oriented programming in Java, data structures, and algorithm design/analysis deliver occupation-specific technical competencies tied to industry standards (Oracle Java certifications, software development practices). Includes performance-based assessments through programming projects rather than solely exams. The Java/OOP/algorithms content maps to recognized postsecondary credentials and software developer career pathway. Preparatory-level CS course is a strong fit for CTE classification. While it could marginally support STEM credit cross-walk, the applied programming focus is clearly CTE-aligned, so keep_target is appropriate.

Linear Algebra for Data Analysis is an academic mathematics course covering matrix operations, linear transformations, eigenvalues, determinants, etc. The "NS" (Natural Science/quantitative) tag and content focus is pure mathematics applied through a programming language as a teaching vehicle. There is no documented Curriculum Framework tying it to a Career Cluster (e.g., STEM cluster industry standards), no Washington State CTE Program Standards alignment, no Leadership/Employability Skills integration, no CTSO linkage (TSA), no exploratory/preparatory CIP designation, no industry credential pathway, and no advisory committee/skills-gap justification. While linear algebra underpins data analytics, this course teaches mathematical theory and intuition — not occupational/technical competencies in a data analyst pathway. It fits HS Math credit (advanced math beyond Algebra II/Precalculus), not CTE.

Independent Study with no defined content, no career cluster alignment, no industry standards, no Curriculum Framework, no leadership/employability skills integration, and no performance assessments specified. The description explicitly states it can be offered in all subjects with topic determined ad hoc between student and instructor. Without a documented CTE Curriculum Framework tying it to one of 16 clusters and OSPI program area standards, it cannot count as CTE. Best classified as a generic Elective since the actual content is undefined.

Web Application Programming aligns to the IT career cluster (Web & Digital Communications / Programming & Software Development pathway). Course delivers occupational/technical competencies: industry-standard backend languages, frontend frameworks, relational databases, REST API design, and codebase organization. These are applied, performance-based skills (build and deploy a working web app) rather than pure academic content, matching OSPI Skilled & Technical Sciences / STEM CTE expectations and supporting progression toward recognized IT credentials.

Software Development Tools aligns clearly with the IT career cluster. Course teaches industry-standard technical competencies (editors with AI tooling, version control/Git, SSH/Linux administration, CI/CD pipelines) that map directly to occupational software development skills and recognized industry practices. Hands-on tool configuration and deployment to production environments constitute performance-based skill demonstrations rather than purely academic assessment. Content supports Preparatory pathway toward postsecondary IT credentials/employment, fitting Skilled & Technical Sciences program area under OSPI. Keep CTE classification.

Course aligns with IT career cluster (Skilled & Technical Sciences / STEM program area). Heavy focus on employability skills (resume, portfolio, professional persona, networking, interview prep) and career pathway exploration directly maps to CTE Core Employability Skills and Leadership Skills strands. Internship/mentorship/community-based learning components reflect real-world industry context. Functions as a Preparatory CTE seminar tied to computer science occupational pathway, not a general college-success course since it is cluster-specific to IT careers and ethical/professional responsibilities in the field.

Course aligns with IT and STEM career clusters through Java programming, data structures, algorithms, and machine architecture - core IT cluster technical competencies. The "(NS)" designation and theoretical computing content also provides academic rigor that can satisfy Math credit (computer science is explicitly recognized as math-equivalent under WA state policy). Lacks explicit leadership/employability/CTSO framing typical of K-12 CTE, but college-level CS courses commonly serve as CTE-equivalent for IT pathway. Conservative add: CTE (IT cluster) + Math.

Computer Organization and Architecture aligns with the IT / STEM career clusters. Course delivers occupation-specific technical competencies (bare-metal C and assembly programming, microprocessor components, fetch/decode/execute cycle, pipelining) that map to industry standards in computer engineering/embedded systems pathways. Hands-on programming work serves as performance assessment rather than purely academic exam content. Fits Skilled & Technical Sciences / STEM program area for HS CTE credit.

Databases and Information Management Systems aligns directly with the IT career cluster (Information Support & Services / Programming & Software Development pathways). Content delivers occupation-specific technical competencies: relational/NoSQL/graph database differentiation, ER data modeling, normalization, SQL CRUD operations, and database-driven application design — all mapped to industry standards (SQL, cloud database platforms) and supporting recognized credentials (Oracle, Microsoft SQL, AWS/Azure database certifications). Hands-on design/implementation deliverables satisfy CTE performance-assessment requirements (labs/projects/technical skill demonstrations) rather than exams alone. This is a Preparatory IT cluster course, not a general "Intro to Computers" exclusion. Keep CTE.

Data Structures and Algorithms aligns with the IT career cluster (Information Support & Services / Programming & Software Development pathway) and STEM cluster. Content (algorithm analysis, self-balancing trees, graph traversal, dynamic programming) maps to industry-recognized software development competencies and supports progression toward postsecondary computing credentials. Hands-on implementation work satisfies performance-assessment expectations. While the course is academically rigorous and could arguably support STEM, it is clearly preparatory CTE coursework in the IT cluster, so keep_target is appropriate.

Advanced CS course covering algorithms, network flow, computational geometry, linear programming, and parallel programming aligns with the STEM career cluster and IT cluster software development pathway (CIP 11.0701). While theoretical, applied problem-solving in algorithms is a recognized CTE pathway toward software engineering careers. Marginal but defensible as CTE.

Software Engineering & Capstone aligns with the IT Career Cluster (Programming & Software Development pathway) and STEM cluster. Course delivers occupation-specific technical competencies (system requirements analysis, Agile/Scrum/Kanban methodologies, DevOps, testing, software development tools) that map to industry-recognized practices. Capstone with real stakeholders provides performance assessment via project demonstration rather than exams, and team collaboration develops Core Employability and Leadership Skills in real-world business/industry contexts. This is Preparatory CTE content tied to a recognized postsecondary/career pathway in software development.

Software Development Practicum applies programming/software development skills to a hands-on real-world project, aligning with the IT career cluster (Skilled & Technical Sciences / STEM program area). Practicum format delivers occupational/technical competencies and project-based performance assessment consistent with Preparatory CTE designation. Reasonable fit for HS CTE credit.

Algorithmic Problem Solving aligns with the IT career cluster and STEM program area. Mock interview format provides performance-based assessment of technical skills plus employability skills (professional communication, interview readiness) in real-world industry contexts. Direct workforce preparation for CS positions satisfies skills-gap/career-pathway justification. The "(NS)" non-standard designation and applied interview-prep framing distinguish this from pure academic CS transfer courses. Preparatory CTE course supporting progression toward IT/software development careers.

CS402 Statistical Methods for User Research is an applied statistics course directly tied to the user research/UX practice within the IT and STEM career clusters. Content (sample sizing, significance testing, margins of error, relationships among variables) delivers occupation-specific technical competencies used by UX researchers, data analysts, and product/IT professionals — not general-ed math transfer. The applied "user research studies" framing supports performance-based assessments (designing studies, computing/interpreting results) consistent with CTE preparatory coursework in IT/STEM pathways. Lean keep_target as it aligns with Skilled & Technical Sciences / STEM CTE strands.

Theory of Computation aligns with the IT/STEM career clusters covering foundational computer science competencies (automata, Turing machines, computability, complexity). While theoretical in nature, it supports the IT cluster's progression toward postsecondary CS credentials and occupational pathways in software engineering. Conservative call to keep CTE classification given clear cluster alignment.

CS422 Principles of Computer Systems covers design, implementation, and evaluation of operating systems and networking — core technical competencies in the IT career cluster (Information Support & Services, Network Systems pathways). Content aligns with industry-recognized standards (CompTIA Network+/Server+, Cisco) and OSPI's Skilled & Technical Sciences / STEM program areas. Hands-on systems work supports performance-based assessment expectations of CTE. Keep as CTE.

Programming Languages aligns with the IT career cluster (Information Support & Services / Programming & Software Development pathway).
