It delivers occupation-specific technical competencies in software development with hands-on experience across language families, supporting progression toward industry-recognized credentials. Fits Skilled & Technical Sciences / STEM program areas. Keep as CTE.

CS440 aligns with the IT career cluster (and arguably STEM), covering AI fundamentals (Search, Logic, Planning, Uncertainty, Reasoning, Learning, Applications) that map to industry-relevant technical competencies. As a 400-level applied computer science course focused on AI techniques and applications, it delivers occupational/technical content consistent with IT pathway preparation. While the description does not explicitly cite OSPI Curriculum Framework, leadership/employability skills, or CTSO integration, the technical/applied nature of AI coursework supports CTE classification under IT cluster. Conservative call: keep CTE.

Capstone Project 2 is a continuation of CS 350, applied computer science project delivered to stakeholders. Aligns with IT career cluster (Skilled & Technical Sciences / STEM program area), demonstrates preparatory occupational competencies, performance assessment via project delivery, and embeds employability skills (stakeholder communication, project management). Fits CTE classification.

CS&141 Computer Science I (Java) aligns with the IT/STEM career clusters through applied programming competencies (problem-solving methodology, Java programming concepts) that map to industry-recognized software development pathways. It supports preparatory CTE designation toward postsecondary IT credentials. The MATH& 141 prerequisite and "Natural Science" framing, plus the computational/algorithmic rigor of an introductory programming course, also commonly qualify for math credit equivalency under WA SBE rules (CS courses are explicitly allowed as third-year math equivalents per RCW 28A.230.097). Keeping CTE and adding Math reflects both the technical/career cluster alignment and the recognized math equivalency.

Introduction to Sonography aligns directly with the Health Science career cluster (one of the 16 national CTE clusters) and OSPI's Health Sciences program area. The course introduces students to the diagnostic medical sonography profession, including sonographer roles, professional organizations, accreditation, and registry significance — all of which map to industry-defined standards (e.g., ARDMS, JRC-DMS, CAAHEP accreditation). This is a classic Exploratory CTE course that provides occupational orientation and career-pathway exposure toward a recognized postsecondary credential (RDMS). Health Science exploratory introductions like this are routinely approved as HS CTE credit when offered through CHS/Running Start. Lean keep_target.

Diagnostic Medical Sonography patient care course aligns with Health Science career cluster. Covers occupation-specific professional/employability skills (communication, empathy, professionalism, work ethic, time management, dealing with criticism) directly applied to the sonographer role on a medical team, plus clinical patient assessment across cultural/age/special-needs populations. This is preparatory Health Sciences CTE content tied to a recognized postsecondary credential pathway (sonography), not general academic coursework.

Diagnostic Medical Sonography Technique Lab I aligns directly with the Health Science career cluster — hands-on scanning, knobology, anatomical recognition, and image acquisition are occupation-specific technical competencies tied to industry standards (ARDMS/CAAHEP sonography credentials). Performance-based lab assessments and progression toward a recognized postsecondary credential satisfy CTE Preparatory criteria. The lab-based applied anatomy and physics content also reasonably supports a Lab Science credit cross-walk, so add_other via union with Science (Lab) is appropriate.

Diagnostic Medical Sonography lab course aligns with Health Science career cluster and Skilled & Technical Sciences program area. Hands-on scanning skills, knobology, anatomy recognition, image acquisition, and ergonomics are occupation-specific technical competencies tied to sonography industry standards (ARDMS/CAAHEP) with performance-based skill demonstrations - core CTE preparatory pathway content.

Diagnostic Medical Sonography lab course aligns with Health Science career cluster and Skilled & Technical Sciences program area. Hands-on scanning skills, image acquisition, ergonomics, and anatomy recognition are occupation-specific technical competencies tied to industry credentialing (ARDMS). Performance-based lab assessments and progression toward a recognized postsecondary credential fit CTE Preparatory designation.

Diagnostic Medical Sonography aligns with Health Science career cluster; teaches occupation-specific technical skills (scanning techniques, sonographic interpretation) toward a recognized postsecondary credential (ARDMS). Preparatory CTE with performance-based assessments.

DMS (Diagnostic Medical Sonography) 112 Cross Sectional Anatomy is occupation-specific coursework within the Health Science career cluster, aligned to the Skilled & Technical Sciences / Health Sciences OSPI program area. It teaches sectional anatomy required for sonography/imaging credentials (ARDMS), supporting a recognized postsecondary credential pathway. This is preparatory CTE content tied to industry-defined competencies for medical imaging professionals, not general academic anatomy.

Diagnostic Medical Sonography course aligned to Health Science career cluster. Preparatory occupation-specific training covering anatomy/physiology, sonographic pathology, scanning protocols, clinical application, and reporting — all industry-standard sonographer competencies leading toward ARDMS credentialing. Includes performance-based clinical skill demonstration. Strong CTE Health Sciences fit; not adding Lab Science because the framing is occupational/technical rather than general academic science.

Abdominal Sonography II is a clear Health Science cluster course within Diagnostic Medical Sonography, a recognized allied health occupation. It builds technical scanning skills, image interpretation, and applied pathophysiology - occupational competencies tied to industry standards (ARDMS/CAAHEP/JRC-DMS) and leads to a recognized postsecondary credential. Aligns with OSPI Health Sciences program area as Preparatory CTE.

DMS120 Ultrasound Physics and Instrumentation I is part of the Diagnostic Medical Sonography program, aligning directly with the Health Science career cluster and OSPI's Health Sciences program area. The course delivers occupation-specific technical competencies (ultrasound physics, instrumentation, image optimization, patient safety) tied to industry standards (ARDMS/SDMS credentialing pathway) and progresses toward a recognized postsecondary credential in sonography. This is a Preparatory CTE course with clear workforce-need justification and clinical/technical skill emphasis rather than general academic content.

Diagnostic Medical Sonography (DMS) program course aligns with Health Science career cluster. Ultrasound Physics and Instrumentation II delivers occupation-specific technical competencies (sonography physics, instrumentation, image optimization, patient safety) tied to ARDMS/ARRT industry credentials. Preparatory course within a recognized postsecondary credential pathway leading to certified sonographer roles. Clear CTE alignment under Health Sciences program area.

Diagnostic Medical Sonography course aligns with Health Science career cluster. Covers occupation-specific technical competencies (ultrasound scanning techniques, image interpretation, anatomy/pathology) leading to a recognized postsecondary credential (sonographer certification - ARDMS pathway). Preparatory CTE course with clear industry standards alignment, hands-on technical skill demonstrations, and direct workforce pathway in allied health.

DMS 131 OB Sonography I is part of Olympic College's Diagnostic Medical Sonography program, which aligns directly with the Health Science career cluster under OSPI's Health Sciences program area. The course delivers occupation-specific technical competencies (fetal embryology, sonographic appearance, measurement parameters) tied to industry standards (ARDMS/SDMS) and progresses toward a recognized postsecondary credential (sonographer certification). It is clearly Preparatory CTE with applied performance-based learning (ultrasound scanning skills) and integrates ethics/professional practice consistent with Core Employability Skills. This is a textbook Health Sciences CTE course.

Vascular Sonography is an occupation-specific Health Science course teaching diagnostic medical sonography competencies (Doppler physics, spectral analysis, vascular pathology interpretation). Aligns to the Health Science career cluster and OSPI Health Sciences program area, with direct progression toward a recognized postsecondary credential (ARDMS vascular technology certification). Preparatory CTE designation fits.

Vascular Sonography Lab aligns to the Health Science career cluster (Skilled & Technical Sciences program area). It is occupation-specific (Preparatory), develops technical sonography competencies via hands-on lab performance (knobology, scanning, producing vascular sonograms), and progresses toward recognized postsecondary credentials (ARDMS/CCI vascular sonography). Performance-based skill demonstration in a lab setting satisfies CTE assessment requirements rather than general academic content.

Diagnostic Medical Sonography Clinical Preparation aligns with Health Science cluster - prepares students for clinical externship with patient care, interpersonal skills (employability), and clinical handbook review. Preparatory course in occupation-specific DMS pathway leading to recognized credential (RDMS). Includes observation/clinical site experience (performance-based).

Advanced OB sonography course aligns squarely with Health Science career cluster and OSPI Health Sciences program area. Covers occupation-specific diagnostic medical sonography competencies tied to ARDMS/SDMS industry standards, emphasizes clinical competence and performance-based assessment, and is preparatory coursework toward a recognized postsecondary credential (RDMS). Classic Health Sciences CTE alignment.

Diagnostic Medical Sonography (DMS) course aligned to Health Science career cluster. Prepares students for ARDMS national registry exam (recognized postsecondary credential), covers ultrasound physics/instrumentation specific to sonographic practice. Clear industry-standard alignment, preparatory occupation-specific content, and credentialing pathway — core CTE Health Sciences strands.

Diagnostic Medical Sonography clinical preceptorship aligns with Health Science career cluster, delivers occupation-specific technical competencies (ultrasound technology, patient care, image interpretation), uses performance-based assessment in real clinical settings, and progresses toward a recognized postsecondary credential (ARDMS sonographer certification). Classic Preparatory CTE in Health Sciences program area.

Diagnostic Medical Sonography subspecialties course aligns with Health Science career cluster (Skilled & Technical Sciences / Health Sciences OSPI program area). Covers occupation-specific technical competencies (neonatal/pediatric sonographic anatomy, pathology assessment, patient case studies) tied to ARDMS/sonography industry credentialing pathway. Preparatory CTE course with clinical/performance assessments, not general academic content.

Diagnostic Medical Sonography clinical practicum aligns with Health Science career cluster and Skilled & Technical Sciences program area. Hands-on hospital/clinical experience with ultrasound instrumentation delivers occupation-specific technical competencies (Preparatory CTE). Includes medical/legal/professional ethics covering employability skills in real-world industry context, and progresses toward recognized postsecondary credential (ARDMS sonographer certification). Performance assessment via clinical skill demonstrations rather than exams. Clear CTE alignment.

Sonographic Analysis is a Diagnostic Medical Sonography course aligned to Health Science cluster (Skilled & Technical Sciences / Health Sciences program area). Includes didactic + clinical + image interpretation with performance-based case presentations - meeting technical competency, industry standards (ARDMS/CAAHEP), and progression toward sonographer credential. Preparatory CTE.

Diagnostic Medical Sonography aligns with the Health Science career cluster and OSPI Health Sciences program area. Course delivers occupation-specific technical competencies (sonographic interpretation, clinical correlation), performance assessments (clinical case presentations, capstone project), and employability skills (written/oral communication, critical thinking in clinical practice). Preparatory pathway toward ARDMS credential. Clear CTE alignment.

Diagnostic Medical Sonography clinical preceptorship aligns directly with the Health Science career cluster and Skilled & Technical Sciences program area. The course delivers preparatory occupation-specific competencies through real-world medical-setting practicum, performance-based technical skill demonstrations (sonographic studies/procedures), and progression toward a recognized postsecondary credential (ARDMS sonographer certification). This matches CTE's required clinical/industry-standards alignment.

Diagnostic Medical Sonography seminar aligns directly with Health Science career cluster (Skilled & Technical Sciences program area). Prepares students for ARDMS national industry credentialing (abdominal, OB/GYN, SPI Physics), which is a recognized postsecondary certification — a core CTE requirement. Includes patient care, clinical correlation, critical thinking, and performance-based assessment via mock exams. Clearly preparatory (occupation-specific) tied to sonographer pathway. Strong CTE fit.

Diagnostic Medical Sonography clinical preceptorship aligns directly with Health Science career cluster (OSPI Health Sciences program area). Course delivers occupation-specific technical competencies (sonographic studies/procedures), performance-based assessments (clinical competencies, terminal program completion), and leads to a recognized postsecondary credential (ARDMS sonographer certification). This is preparatory CTE tied to industry standards (CAAHEP/JRC-DMS), satisfying the skills-gap and workforce-need criteria.

Early Childhood Education aligns with the Education & Training career cluster and OSPI's Family & Consumer Sciences program area. Content on professional qualifications, ethical practices, and interpersonal/workplace skills directly maps to CTE Core Employability Skills and Leadership Skills in a real-world early-learning industry context. ECE coursework is part of a recognized preparatory pathway toward CDA/state ECE certification, satisfying the progression-to-credential strand.

Early Childhood Education Practicum II is a hands-on, occupation-specific work experience in the Education & Training career cluster (FCS/Human Services pathway). It applies ECE theories/methods in a real childcare/preschool setting, satisfies preparatory CTE criteria (real-world industry context, performance-based assessment via supervised practicum, progression toward ECE credentials/CDA/AA-T), and aligns with OSPI Family & Consumer Sciences program area. Prereqs in the ECED& sequence confirm pathway scaffolding.

ECED164 is part of Olympic College's Early Childhood Education program, which aligns to the Education & Training national career cluster and OSPI's CTE framework. The course teaches pedagogical methods for delivering math concepts in early learning settings — occupation-specific content for the ECE workforce, not pure academic math transfer. It supports a recognized career pathway leading to credentials (CDA, ECE certificates/AAS), connects to the Educators Rising CTSO, and addresses a documented WA early learning workforce skills gap. The MATH 090 prerequisite confirms it's applied methods rather than a college-level math transfer course (which would not satisfy core math credit anyway). Lean keep_target as it fits the Education & Training cluster preparatory pathway.

Early Childhood Education course using ECERS industry-standard assessment tool aligns with Human Services cluster and FCS program area. Applied technical skill (environment evaluation) supports preparatory ECE pathway toward recognized credentials (CDA, State ECE certificates).

Early Childhood Education courses align with the Education & Training career cluster under OSPI's CTE framework. ECED176 covers physical/motor development sequencing and applied music/movement activities for early learning environments — these are occupation-specific competencies for early childhood educators/paraprofessionals, not general academic content. The course directly supports the ECE pathway leading to recognized credentials (Washington State STARS training, CDA, AAS in ECE) and connects to the Educators Rising CTSO pathway. Content is preparatory (occupation-specific) with applied performance components (activity design, equipment use) rather than purely exam-based. Properly classified as CTE.

ECED177 Science for Young Children is an Early Childhood Education course aligning with the Education & Training career cluster (a recognized national cluster) and OSPI's Family & Consumer Sciences program area. ECE coursework addresses occupation-specific competencies (developmentally appropriate practice, curriculum planning, child cognitive development) tied to recognized postsecondary credentials (ECE State Certificate/AAS, CDA credential) and aligns to NAEYC professional standards. The course is part of a preparatory pathway toward early childhood educator/childcare provider careers, satisfying CTE's requirement for industry-aligned technical competencies and real-world application. While general science appreciation would not be CTE, this course is framed within an ECE pathway focused on teaching young children, making it occupationally relevant.

Generic Independent Study with no defined curriculum, career cluster alignment, industry standards, leadership/employability skills, or performance assessments. Course content is undefined and varies per student. Despite ECED prefix suggesting Early Childhood Education, the description explicitly states it "can be offered in all subjects" - no documented Curriculum Framework, no CIP code, no Exploratory/Preparatory designation, no CTSO linkage. Fails core CTE requirements for documented industry standards and measurable technical competencies. Best fit is generic Elective.

Early Childhood Education Practicum III is a clinical/applied course in the Education & Training career cluster (FCS/Human Services program area). Students apply cumulative skills with children and engage in professional interactions with families and staff in a real early childhood setting — direct industry-context application. The 12-hour clinic and prerequisite sequence (ECED& 120, ECED 151) indicate a preparatory pathway aligned to recognized ECE credentials (CDA, state Initial/Short Cert), with embedded employability skills and performance-based assessment. This is a textbook CTE preparatory course.

ECE Professional Portfolio aligns with the Education & Training career cluster (Early Learning Services pathway) under OSPI's Family & Consumer Sciences / Education program area. The course requires students to compile and evaluate professional ePortfolio documentation of key assessments from ECE coursework and plan professional improvement goals — these are preparatory, occupation-specific employability and professional-development competencies tied to ECE workforce credentials (e.g., Washington STARS, CDA, ECE Initial/Short Certificate stackable credentials). Portfolio assessment is a recognized CTE performance assessment (not exam/essay only), and the seminar directly addresses Core Employability Skills and career-pathway progression. Keep as CTE.

ECED220 fits the Education & Training career cluster and OSPI Family & Consumer Sciences / Education pathway. Early Childhood Education is a recognized CTE preparatory pathway leading to credentials (CDA, state ECE certificates). While this specific course is theory/history focused, it is a foundational course in an ECE CTE sequence that ties to industry standards (NAEYC) and workforce needs. Keep CTE classification.

Early Childhood Education aligns with the Education & Training career cluster and OSPI's Family & Consumer Sciences program area. ECED courses are part of the ECE career pathway leading to the Washington State Stackable Certificate and CDA credential, addressing workforce needs in early learning. While this is a topical/issues course rather than a hands-on lab, it functions as part of the preparatory ECE sequence supporting industry-recognized credentials, making CTE classification defensible.

ECED&100 aligns with the Education & Training career cluster and OSPI's Family & Consumer Sciences / Human Services program areas. It delivers occupational competencies (child growth/development, guidance, health/safety/nutrition, professional practice) tied to a recognized industry credential pathway (Washington STARS 30-hour Basics, MERIT system registration for licensed early learning providers). This is a preparatory CTE course leading to state-recognized early childhood education certification, satisfying the workforce-credential and industry-standard requirements of HS CTE.

ECED&105 aligns with the Education & Training career cluster and Human Services cluster (Early Childhood Development pathway). Early Childhood Education is a recognized OSPI-approved CTE program area under Family & Consumer Sciences (FCS), with CIP code alignment (19.0709 Child Care Provider/Assistant or 13.1210 Early Childhood Education and Teaching). The course covers program models, best practices, and professional observation - foundational competencies tied to NAEYC industry standards and the ECE career pathway leading to credentials like the CDA (Child Development Associate). Course connects to FCCLA CTSO and preparatory pathway toward postsecondary ECE credentials. Despite the "Social Science" label in the description, ECED& prefix courses are part of the statewide ECE pathway commonly articulated for HS CTE credit.

ECED&107 Health/Safety/Nutrition is a core Early Childhood Education course aligning to the Human Services career cluster (Early Childhood Education and Services pathway) and OSPI's Family & Consumer Sciences program area. It delivers occupation-specific competencies (child health, nutrition, safety protocols, mandated reporter training for abuse/neglect) required for the Washington State Early Achievers/STARS credential and CDA pathway — recognized postsecondary credentials. Content maps to NAEYC standards and state child care licensing requirements (industry-defined standards), with applied/performance-based learning rather than pure academic content. Fits Preparatory CTE with FCCLA CTSO alignment.

ECED&120 is an applied practicum in early childhood education that aligns directly with the Education & Training career cluster and OSPI's Family & Consumer Sciences program area. The course delivers occupation-specific competencies (child health/safety, supportive relationships) tied to NAEYC industry standards and WA Early Learning Core Competencies, uses performance-based assessment in a real early learning setting, and progresses toward recognized credentials (CDA, WA State Initial Certificate). Practicum-based ECE is a textbook Preparatory CTE course with FCCLA CTSO alignment available.

Early Childhood Education aligns with the Human Services career cluster and OSPI's Family & Consumer Sciences program area. ECED courses are part of the Early Care and Education stackable certificate pathway leading to recognized credentials (Washington State Initial/Short Certificate, CDA). Content covers caregiver role, developmentally appropriate practices, and family relationships—core occupational competencies for the childcare workforce with clear skills-gap justification. FCCLA is the aligned CTSO. This is a preparatory CTE course, not general academic content.

ECED&134 Family Child Care aligns with OSPI's Family & Consumer Sciences / Human Services / Education & Training CTE program areas. Covers licensing, business management, health/safety/nutrition, child guidance, and growth/development — occupation-specific competencies for in-home childcare providers. Maps to a recognized career pathway (Early Childhood Education) with industry standards (WA DCYF licensing) and progression toward STARS/CDA credentials. Preparatory CTE course with clear workforce alignment.

ECED& courses are Early Childhood Education, which aligns to the Education & Training career cluster (one of the 16 national clusters) and OSPI's Education & Training pathway. The course covers teacher role, supporting learning environments, and pedagogical practices for early childhood educators — these are occupation-specific competencies for the ECE workforce, not general academic content. ECE coursework progresses toward state-recognized credentials (Washington State Initial/Short/State ECE Certificates stackable to AA) and connects to the Educators Rising CTSO pathway. The applied teacher-practice focus (adult comfort, risk-taking, environment design) constitutes performance-based CTE content rather than academic transfer material. Keep as CTE.

ECED&138 aligns with the Human Services cluster (Early Childhood Development & Services pathway) and OSPI's Family & Consumer Sciences program area. Content delivers occupation-specific competencies for home visitors and ECE professionals, supports the CDA credential pathway, applies NAEYC and WA Early Learning Guidelines as industry standards, and includes applied performance tasks (planning/providing home visits and group activities). Preparatory CTE course with clear postsecondary credential progression — keep CTE classification.

ECED&139 aligns clearly with the Education & Training career cluster and OSPI's Family & Consumer Sciences program area. The course delivers occupation-specific competencies for early childhood program administration: licensing compliance, NAEYC standard alignment, program operations, and assessment - all tied to industry/nationally-defined standards (NAEYC). It is Preparatory toward recognized credentials (ECE Initial/Short/State Certificates, CDA pathway) and supports workforce-need justification in the early learning sector. Administrative content (developing, opening, operating, managing programs) reflects real-world business/industry context and employability skills central to CTE. No stronger alternate credit type applies.

ECED&160 Curriculum Development is an Early Childhood Education course aligning with the Education & Training career cluster and OSPI's Family & Consumer Sciences program area. It addresses occupation-specific competencies (learning theory, program planning, curriculum design for young children's motor/social-emotional/cognitive/creative development) directly tied to the ECE pathway and recognized credentials (Washington State ECE Initial/Short Certificate stackable to AAS, leading to CDA). Content is applied/preparatory rather than general-ed, supporting workforce entry in childcare/preschool settings—a documented WA skills-gap area. Keep CTE.

ECED&170 is an Early Childhood Education course directly aligned with the Education & Training career cluster and Family & Consumer Sciences (FCS) OSPI program area. It teaches applied technical competencies (designing/evaluating early learning environments) tied to NAEYC industry standards, fits the ECE preparatory pathway leading to recognized credentials (Initial/Short Cert, AAS, CDA), and supports FCCLA/Educators Rising CTSO linkage. Content is occupation-specific rather than general academic.

ECED&180 is part of the Early Childhood Education program, which aligns to the Education & Training career cluster (OSPI Family & Consumer Sciences program area). It delivers occupation-specific competencies for early childhood educators/paraeducators — language acquisition strategies and literacy skill development across developmental stages — directly tied to NAEYC and WA State Early Learning standards used in the ECE pathway leading to the State Initial Certificate and CDA credential. This is preparatory CTE coursework, not general academic content.

ECED&190 Observation/Assessment is part of the Early Childhood Education (ECED) program, which aligns directly to the Education & Training career cluster and Human Services cluster under OSPI's CTE program areas (Family & Consumer Sciences). The course teaches occupation-specific competencies for early childhood educators: collecting/recording observation and assessment data, planning to support children/families, reflection techniques, and professional communication of data - all core technical skills for the ECE workforce tied to WA STARS training and Washington State Core Competencies for Early Care and Education Professionals. ECED courses at WA community colleges are explicitly designed as preparatory CTE coursework leading to recognized credentials (Initial/Short Certificate, ECE Certificate, AAS), with embedded employability skills and performance-based assessments. This is a legitimate CTE classification.

Academic-transfer Social Science course explicitly flagged in CTE exclusion list. Description self-labels as "Social Science" covering theoretical microeconomic concepts (supply/demand, market structures, antitrust) without Finance/Business cluster pathway framing, industry standards (no NAF), documented CTE Curriculum Framework, leadership/employability skills integration, performance assessments, or CTSO linkage. Fits Social Studies - Elective only.

ECON&202 Macro Economics is an academic-transfer social science course covering national income, employment, inflation, money, and international trade. It lacks the required CTE Curriculum Framework, industry/nationally-defined technical standards (e.g., NAF Finance), Washington State CTE Program Standards alignment, integrated Leadership/Employability Skills, performance-based assessments, CIP code, advisory committee input, and CTSO linkage. The OSPI guidance explicitly lists ECON& 201/202 as NOT CTE absent Finance or Business/Management pathway framing. Best fit is Social Studies - Elective, which it already carries.

Intro to Engineering aligns with STEM cluster and Skilled & Technical Sciences program area. Career exploration of engineering fields fits Exploratory CTE designation, introducing students to engineering pathways and careers. Supports progression toward postsecondary engineering credentials. While light on hands-on lab/performance assessment (Pass/No Credit format), the career-pathway focus matches CTE STEM exploratory framing.

Introductory engineering problem-solving with MATLAB aligns with the STEM career cluster and Skilled & Technical Sciences / STEM program area. It delivers applied technical competencies (computational tools, engineering problem-solving methodology) rather than pure academic math, supporting a preparatory engineering pathway. Reasonable fit for CTE classification.

ENGR195 Independent Study has no fixed curriculum, no documented Curriculum Framework, no industry-defined technical standards, and no required leadership/employability skills integration. Topic and scope are determined ad hoc between student and instructor and the course can cover any subject ("can be offered in all subjects"), so it cannot be reliably aligned to a single Career Cluster or OSPI program area. Without a documented competency set, performance assessments tied to industry standards, or a CIP-coded pathway designation (Exploratory/Preparatory), it fails the OSPI K-12 CTE criteria. Best fit is generic Elective credit.

Advanced CAD course covers industry-standard technical competencies (SolidWorks-style surfaces, sheet metal, weldments, molds, FEA, CFD) directly aligned to STEM/Manufacturing/Architecture & Construction clusters. Skilled & Technical Sciences program area with applied performance assessments (CAD modeling, documentation) and clear progression toward engineering/drafting credentials. Strong CTE fit.

ENGR234 Design of Logic Circuits aligns with the STEM and IT career clusters and Skilled & Technical Sciences program area. It covers industry-relevant technical competencies (combinational/sequential logic design, modern EDA tools) that map to electronics/computer engineering pathways and recognized credentials. The CS& 141 prerequisite indicates applied technical rigor consistent with Preparatory CTE engineering coursework. It also delivers engineering science content that can satisfy Science (Non-Lab) credit as applied engineering/computing science. Keeping CTE is appropriate; adding Science (Non-Lab) reflects its dual academic-engineering rigor. Not classified as Math because the focus is circuit design rather than mathematical proof/computation, and not Lab Science absent confirmation of a structured wet/physical lab component.

Academic-transfer engineering numerical methods course (prereq MATH& 163, co-req Diff Eq) explicitly labeled "Natural Science" in description. No industry certification pathway, no CTE Curriculum Framework, no leadership/employability skills integration, no CTSO linkage, no advisory committee or skills-gap justification. Pure applied computational mathematics for engineering majors. MATLAB programming serves engineering theory, not occupational preparation. Fits Math (applied numerical methods, computational algorithms) and Science (Non-Lab) credit (engineering science without explicit lab component).

ENGR 270 is an academic-transfer engineering science course (common-course-numbered sequence with CHEM& 141 prereq, co-enrollment with ENGR 271 lab, formerly ENGR 170). It teaches fundamental principles of materials structure and properties for engineering majors — pure engineering science content with no documented OSPI CTE Curriculum Framework, no Washington State CTE Program Standards alignment, no integrated Leadership/Employability Skills, no CTSO linkage (SkillsUSA/TSA), no advisory committee oversight, and no occupational/preparatory pathway to a recognized industry credential. While materials science topically overlaps the STEM cluster, OSPI CTE requires more than topical fit — it requires the framework, employability skills, and pathway structure that engineering transfer courses lack. The course does fit Science (Lab) credit: physical science content (chemistry-based materials science) with a co-required lab component (ENGR 271) providing hands-on measurable lab work.

Materials Sciences Laboratory aligns with STEM/Manufacturing CTE clusters through hands-on material testing, stress analysis, and engineering data analysis—delivering applied technical competencies and performance-based assessments rather than pure academic content. The lab format (material testing, experimental stress analysis, report writing) also satisfies Science (Lab) credit. Prerequisite chain with ENGR& 225 confirms engineering pathway sequencing consistent with Preparatory CTE.

Engineering design course aligns with STEM career cluster and Skilled & Technical Sciences program area. Project-based engineering approach with design/communication principles maps to WA OSPI-approved Engineering Design CTE pathway (analogous to PLTW Introduction to Engineering Design, a widely approved HS CTE course). Preparatory toward engineering technology careers with performance-based design assessments.

Engineering Graphics teaches industry-standard sketching and parametric CAD modeling (e.g., SolidWorks/Inventor) applied through team design projects — classic STEM/Skilled & Technical Sciences cluster content with technical skill demonstrations and real-world engineering design workflow, satisfying CTE strands.

ENGR&204 Electrical Circuits is an academic engineering transfer course requiring Calculus-based Physics (PHYS 255) and Math 221 as prerequisites. It teaches theoretical circuit analysis through differential equations - this is engineering science, not K-12 CTE. It lacks CTE hallmarks: no industry certification pathway (e.g., NCCER, electrician licensure prep), no documented WA CTE Curriculum Framework, no Leadership/Employability skill integration, no CTSO linkage (SkillsUSA/TSA), and no exploratory/preparatory CIP designation. The lab component and rigorous math/physics content align it with HS Lab Science credit (engineering science equivalency), comparable to AP Physics C circuits content. While engineering connects to STEM cluster conceptually, this specific course is academic transfer prep for engineering majors, not occupational/technical competency development with employability framing required by OSPI CTE standards.

ENGR&214 Statics is a foundational engineering course that aligns with the STEM career cluster (Engineering & Technology pathway), one of the 16 national CTE clusters. It delivers technical competencies (vector mechanics, force analysis) that progress toward engineering credentials/postsecondary engineering programs, supporting the Skilled & Technical Sciences / STEM program area. It also delivers rigorous applied physics content (Newtonian mechanics) that can satisfy a Science credit, though it is calculation/problem-solving based without a structured lab component, so Science (Non-Lab) is the appropriate science classification. Keeping CTE while adding Science (Non-Lab) reflects its dual nature as an engineering-pathway technical course with science content.

ENGR&215 Dynamics is a pre-professional engineering course aligned to the STEM Career Cluster (Engineering & Technology pathway), one of the 16 national clusters recognized by OSPI. It builds occupation-specific technical competencies (Newtonian mechanics, vector calculus applied to motion, energy/momentum methods) that progress toward a recognized postsecondary engineering credential, satisfying the STEM program area's preparatory designation. It also delivers rigorous applied physics content (kinematics, kinetics, Newton's laws) that can cross-walk to HS Science credit. The description does not mention lab components, so Science (Non-Lab) is the appropriate science designation. Leaning add_other rather than remove_target because engineering coursework is explicitly recognized under the STEM CTE cluster.

ENGR&224 Thermodynamics is an academic engineering transfer course covering energy conservation, energy transformation, and maximum efficiency with calculus/physics prerequisites (MATH& 163 Calculus, PHYS 254). It is a pure theoretical engineering science course in the transfer engineering sequence, not a CTE course. It lacks the defining CTE elements: no documented Curriculum Framework tied to WA CTE Program Standards, no industry-recognized technical credential pathway (no NCCER, NATEF, etc.), no Leadership/Employability Skills integration, no CTSO linkage (SkillsUSA/TSA), no Exploratory/Preparatory designation, no advisory-committee skills-gap justification, and no occupation-specific competencies. While STEM is one of the 16 national Career Clusters, OSPI-approved CTE STEM courses must still include the CTE framework components (employability skills, performance assessments tied to industry standards, advisory input). ENGR&224 is a calculus-based engineering theory course for university transfer, fitting Science (Non-Lab) credit — it covers scientific principles (thermodynamics) but the description indicates no lab component (unlike ENGR& courses that pair with lab sections). Best fit is Science (Non-Lab).

Survey of Accounting aligns with the Business/Management and Finance career clusters and OSPI's Business & Marketing program area. Course explicitly emphasizes applications to career programs (Management, CIS), covers industry-standard technical competencies (double-entry accounting, financial statements, accruals/deferrals, internal controls, receivables/payables) that map to GAAP and FBLA/DECA accounting pathways. This is a classic preparatory CTE course tied to recognized postsecondary credentials (bookkeeping/accounting clerk certifications, CPA pathway). Keep as CTE.

QuickBooks aligns directly with the Business/Management and Finance career clusters under OSPI's Business & Marketing program area. It delivers occupation-specific technical competencies (software navigation, chart of accounts customization, AR/AP file management, sales transaction recording, banking activities) tied to industry-standard accounting software widely used in business. Outcomes are performance-based (navigate, customize, process) rather than purely academic, supporting a Preparatory designation toward bookkeeping/accounting clerk pathways and recognized credentials (QuickBooks Certified User). Naturally supports FBLA/DECA CTSO integration and employability skills in real-world business context.

Excel for Accounting aligns with the Business/Management and Finance career clusters (OSPI Business & Marketing program area). It delivers occupation-specific technical competencies (journal entries, financial statements, inventory costing, bank reconciliations, payroll registers, budgeting/cost analysis) using industry-standard Excel tools, which maps to recognized accounting/business credentials. Performance is demonstrated through applied Excel projects rather than essays, satisfying CTE's performance-assessment requirement. Prerequisite of ACCT& 201/ACCT 101 confirms preparatory pathway sequencing toward accounting occupations. Fits CTE classification.

Federal Income Tax Preparation aligns with the Finance and Business/Management career clusters under OSPI's Business & Marketing program area. Course delivers occupation-specific technical competencies (preparing 1040/1040A/1040EZ forms, schedules, filing statuses, capital gains, deductions, credits) tied to industry tax-preparation practice, supporting pathways to recognized credentials (e.g., IRS AFSP/Enrolled Agent, NAF Finance pathway). This is preparatory CTE content, not general academic transfer.

Payroll and Business Taxes is occupation-specific accounting coursework aligning with the Business/Management and Finance career clusters under OSPI's Business & Marketing program area. Content covers payroll preparation, tax law compliance (FLSA), required federal/state tax returns, and uses both manual and computerized payroll systems—delivering technical competencies tied to industry standards (AIPB/APA payroll certifications) and real-world employability skills. This is preparatory CTE, not general-ed academic content.

Nonprofit financial management/reporting is an applied accounting course in the Business/Management & Finance cluster (OSPI Business & Marketing program area). Covers occupational competencies: GAAP for nonprofits, Form 990/1023 IRS compliance, fundraising/grants management, governance — all tied to industry-defined standards (FASB, IRS). Prerequisite ACCT& 201 confirms preparatory accounting sequence aligned to recognized postsecondary/credential pathways (CPA, nonprofit finance roles). Fits CTE as preparatory occupational coursework.

Accounting Information Systems aligns directly with the Business/Management and Finance career clusters under OSPI's Business & Marketing program area. Course content covers AIS design, business process documentation, control/governance frameworks for risk assessment, and internal controls implementation - all occupation-specific technical competencies tied to accounting industry standards (AICPA, COSO frameworks). This is preparatory CTE coursework supporting progression toward recognized credentials (CPA pathway, bookkeeping certifications) and aligns with FBLA/DECA CTSO pathways. Builds on ACCT&201 prerequisite, indicating sequential career pathway rather than general academic transfer.

Income Tax Practicum aligns directly with the Finance career cluster and Business & Marketing OSPI program area. Prepares actual tax returns under approved mentorship (AARP Tax-Aide) — clear preparatory designation with real-world industry context, performance-based assessment (analyzing client documents, preparing returns), and progression toward recognized credential (IRS VITA/AARP certification). Includes occupational technical competencies (classification of reportable items, deductions, credits) not just academic content.

Principles of Accounting I aligns directly with the Finance and Business/Management Career Clusters under OSPI's Business & Marketing program area. It teaches occupation-specific technical competencies (recording business transactions, GAAP-based financial statement preparation, accounting cycle) that map to industry standards (AICPA, NAF Academy of Finance) and support preparatory pathway sequences toward accounting/business credentials. Accounting is a foundational course in OSPI-approved Business & Marketing CTE sequences and supports DECA/FBLA CTSO linkage. CTE classification is appropriate.

ACCT&202 Principles of Accounting II aligns with the Finance and Business/Management career clusters under OSPI's Business & Marketing program area. Content (partnerships, bonds, corporations, cash flow statements, financial statement analysis) maps to industry-recognized accounting competencies (GAAP, financial reporting) consistent with NAF Finance pathway and AICPA standards. It builds occupational/technical competencies beyond general academic content and supports preparatory career pathway sequencing toward accounting credentials (CPA pathway, bookkeeping certifications). While also a transfer course, it delivers the technical skills required for a CTE designation. Conservative call: keep_target.

Managerial accounting (job order/process costing, budgeting, CVP, standard costs) aligns directly with the Business, Management & Administration and Finance career clusters under OSPI's Business & Marketing program area. Content delivers occupational/technical accounting competencies used in industry (cost accounting, budgeting, capital budgeting) consistent with NBEA/AICPA-aligned standards and supports preparatory pathway sequences leading to bookkeeping/accounting credentials. Appropriate CTE classification; not a general-ed humanities or pure academic transfer course.

BUS134 Introduction to Social Media aligns with the Business/Management and Marketing career clusters under OSPI's Business & Marketing program area. Content covers how businesses evaluate and use social media, content authoring for business tools (blogs, podcasts, RSS, video), community engagement, and influencer identification — all occupational/technical competencies tied to digital marketing and business communications industry practice. Supports CTSO linkages (DECA, FBLA) and pathway progression toward digital marketing/social media management credentials. Appropriate as Preparatory CTE.

BUS135 Introduction to Online Marketing aligns directly with OSPI CTE standards under the Marketing Career Cluster and OSPI Business & Marketing program area. Content addresses industry-relevant competencies: SEO, social media marketing, online advertising strategies (PPC, banner ads, email marketing), and web presence analysis - all core Marketing cluster skills tied to digital marketing occupations. Course uses real industry platforms (Facebook, LinkedIn, Twitter) in applied contexts, supports DECA/FBLA CTSO linkage, and develops occupational competencies aligned to recognized digital marketing pathways. Includes practical skill development (Microsoft Word/PowerPoint, LMS proficiency, developing online marketing materials) consistent with Exploratory CTE designation. This is not pure academic theory - it delivers technical marketing competencies appropriate for HS CTE credit.

BUS240 Human Relations in the Workplace aligns with the Business/Management Career Cluster and OSPI Business & Marketing program area. Content (organizational behavior, leadership, teamwork, communication, conflict resolution, motivation) directly addresses CTE Core Leadership Skills and Core Employability Skills applied in real-world business contexts. Course is framed around business/organizational needs rather than as a pure psychology transfer course, supporting Exploratory CTE designation in the Business pathway.

Project Management I aligns directly with the Business/Management Career Cluster under OSPI's Business & Marketing program area. The course teaches industry-standard project management vocabulary, tools, and technology skills (PMI-aligned), scheduling, collaboration/communication in a project environment, and professional ethics — all occupational/technical competencies. Prerequisites in CIS/BTECA indicate a sequenced career pathway. Use of project simulation and case studies provides performance-based assessment rather than purely academic exams. Employability skills (communication, collaboration, ethics) are integrated in real-world business contexts, satisfying CTE Core Leadership/Employability requirements. Progression toward recognized credentials (CAPM/PMP) is supported. Conservative call: clear Business & Marketing CTE alignment.

Project Management II aligns with the Business/Management career cluster and OSPI's Business & Marketing program area. Course explicitly references industry standards (PMI/PMBOK terminology), technical project management vocabulary, technology skills, communication, decision-making, leadership, and strategic alignment — all core CTE leadership/employability competencies applied in real-world business contexts. Use of simulation/case studies satisfies the performance-assessment requirement (not solely exams/essays). Sequenced with BUS 260 prerequisite, supporting a Preparatory pathway toward PMI CAPM/PMP credentials. Strong fit for CTE; no other credit type applies.

BUS279 Personal Finance aligns with the Finance career cluster and OSPI Business & Marketing program area. Content covers occupational competencies (budgeting, income tax, cash management, loans, insurance, investments, mutual funds, retirement, estate planning) consistent with NAF/JumpStart financial literacy standards and FBLA pathway topics. The course delivers applied financial decision-making skills relevant to real-world business/consumer contexts, supporting both Exploratory designation and employability skill development. Personal Finance is commonly approved as HS CTE under the Finance cluster in WA OSPI's course equivalency frameworks.

BUS&101 Introduction to Business surveys core business functions (operations, marketing, accounting/finance, management/leadership, entrepreneurship, legal structures, international commerce), which directly aligns with the Business/Management and Marketing career clusters under OSPI's Business & Marketing program area. The content provides exploratory exposure to business pathways consistent with HS CTE Intro to Business equivalents and supports FBLA/DECA CTSO linkage. While it is also listed as a Social Sciences transfer course, the content strands map cleanly to CTE business cluster standards, so keeping CTE classification is appropriate.

BUS&201 Business Law aligns with the Business Management & Administration career cluster (one of the 16 national clusters) and falls under OSPI's Business & Marketing program area. Content covers contracts, dispute resolution, court systems, and commercial law — directly relevant to business operations and a recognized component of FBLA/DECA competitive events and business pathway sequences. While it is an academic-transfer course, business law is a standard offering in CTE Business pathways and supports preparatory business/finance career sequences. The Social Studies - Elective designation is also defensible since it carries Social Sciences distribution credit and addresses legal/civic systems. Conservative call: keep both classifications as the course legitimately straddles CTE Business pathway and social studies elective content.

CMST&220 is explicitly named in the guidance as a course that sounds like CTE but is not — it is general-ed speech taught as a Humanities distribution/transfer elective with no career cluster alignment, no industry/national technical standards, no Washington CTE Program Standards crosswalk, no leadership/employability skills framework, no CTSO linkage, no performance assessments tied to industry credentials, and no exploratory/preparatory CIP designation. The course content (speech organization, audience analysis, informative/persuasive speeches, outlines, fact/value/policy claims) is academic communication theory, not Business/Marketing or Arts/AV/Communications production. ELA is retained as marginal support since oral communication aligns with Common Core Speaking & Listening strands, and Elective is added as the safest HS credit fit for a humanities transfer course.

CS202 Computer Science II aligns with the IT career cluster (Programming & Software Development pathway). It delivers occupation-specific technical competencies in Object-Oriented Programming (inheritance, polymorphism, abstraction, encapsulation), data structures, code versioning, and error handling - all industry-standard software engineering skills tied to recognized IT credentials. The applied programming work (classes, objects, recursion, maintainability/reusability techniques) constitutes performance-based technical skill demonstration rather than purely academic content. This is preparatory CTE coursework that progresses toward postsecondary IT credentials and software development careers, distinguishing it from the excluded 'generic intro to computers' courses. Note: while listed as 'General Transfer Elective,' the technical/occupational content matches IT cluster standards.

CS&141 Computer Science I aligns with the IT career cluster and STEM program area. It teaches structured/procedural programming, OOP, debugging, design, and program logic - core technical competencies tied to IT industry standards and a recognized pathway toward postsecondary credentials and certifications. Unlike generic "Intro to Computers" or keyboarding (explicitly flagged as not CTE), this is a rigorous programming course with applied technical skill development via labs/projects (the structured programming work). It fits the Skilled & Technical Sciences / STEM program areas and supports preparatory pathway sequences in software development. Keep as CTE.

ECED (Early Childhood Education) courses align to the Education & Training career cluster and OSPI's Family & Consumer Sciences program area. The course addresses child development (physical, cognitive, emotional, social), developmentally appropriate practice, and family-staff collaboration — core competencies in the Early Learning/ECE pathway tied to industry standards (NAEYC, WA STARS, MERIT) and progressing toward the WA State ECE Initial/Short Certificate stackable credential. Applied work in the campus Child Development Center provides real-world performance assessment rather than purely academic exams. Lean keep_target.

ECED103 Parent and Child is an Early Childhood Education course addressing child growth/development, developmentally appropriate practices, child guidance, and includes lab-school participation plus leadership development opportunities. This aligns to the Human Services cluster (Early Childhood Development pathway) and OSPI's Family & Consumer Sciences program area, with observation/lab components serving as performance assessment and explicit leadership development. ECE coursework is a standard CTE pathway leading toward early childhood credentials (CDA, state ECE certificates). Keep CTE classification.

ECED104 Parent and Child sits within Pierce's Early Childhood Education program, which maps to the Human Services career cluster and OSPI's Family & Consumer Sciences program area. The course delivers occupation-relevant competencies in child development (physical, social, emotional, cognitive) for ages 18-36 months through a child-study laboratory with observation components, satisfying the performance-assessment requirement (lab-based, not exam-only). ECE coursework at Pierce ladders toward state ECE certificates and the Washington ECE Initial/Short Certificate stackable credentials, providing the postsecondary credential progression CTE requires. While "Parent and Child" framing leans toward parent education, as part of the ECED sequence it functions as an exploratory/preparatory ECE course aligned to early-learning industry standards (NAEYC, WA Core Competencies for Early Care and Education Professionals). Conservative call: keep CTE.

ECED225 is part of a Preparatory CTE pathway in Early Childhood Education, aligning with the Education & Training career cluster and FCS program area. The course requires prerequisite ECED& 105 plus concurrent ART/MUSC courses, showing structured pathway sequencing toward CDA/ECE credentials. Content covers applied curriculum development, planning, and assessment for real classroom environments - meeting CTE requirements for occupation-specific competencies and performance assessment rather than pure academic transfer. ECE is a recognized workforce-need pathway with industry-aligned credentials.

Early Childhood Education capstone aligns to Education & Training career cluster (FCS/Education pathway). Planning, curriculum development, assessment of diverse children in a Lab School setting delivers occupation-specific competencies, performance-based assessment, and real-world industry context consistent with OSPI CTE Preparatory designation. Fits Human Services/Education & Training cluster with progression toward ECE credential (CDA/state certification).

Early Childhood Student Teaching is a capstone preparatory practicum in the Education & Training career cluster (OSPI FCS/Education program area). It delivers occupational/technical competencies (lead teaching, curriculum implementation, classroom management) in a real-world lab setting with supervised performance assessment, aligning to ECE industry standards (NAEYC) and a recognized postsecondary pathway (ECE certificate/AAS, state ECE Initial Certificate). Embedded employability and leadership skills via team building and classroom leadership. Strong fit for CTE.

Early Childhood Education capstone in a Child Development Center is occupation-specific Preparatory CTE under Education & Training cluster (FCS program area). Curriculum development, assessment of children, and supervised CDC student teaching deliver applied technical competencies, performance-based assessment, and direct pathway toward ECE credentials (CDA, ECE certificate), aligning to industry standards.

Early Childhood Education student teaching practicum aligns directly with the Education & Training career cluster and OSPI's Family & Consumer Sciences/Skilled & Technical Sciences program areas. Supervised lead teaching in a Child Development Center delivers occupation-specific (Preparatory) competencies, performance-based assessment (real classroom application), and progression toward recognized credentials (Initial/Short Certificate in Early Childhood Education, CDA pathway). Embeds employability and leadership skills in a real-world industry context with advisory-committee oversight typical of ECE programs.

ECED307 prepares future early childhood educators to plan and deliver health/fitness instruction to children birth-grade 3, aligning with the Education & Training career cluster (one of OSPI's 16 clusters). Content addresses WA Health/Fitness Learning Standards, OSPI curriculum resources, instructional planning, and individualizing instruction — occupational competencies for the early-childhood-educator pathway (Preparatory CTE). It is not a personal PE/fitness activity course nor a generic health course; it is pedagogy-focused teacher preparation, which fits Education/Training cluster framing.

ECED325 is part of Pierce's BAS-T (Teacher Education) program, an Early Childhood Education pathway. It aligns with the Education & Training career cluster (one of the 16 national clusters) and OSPI's Education & Training program area. The course develops occupation-specific competencies for early childhood educators (math pedagogy for birth-grade 3), addresses culturally/linguistically/ability-diverse learners (real-world workforce context), and ties to state early learning guidelines (industry-relevant standards for ECE practitioners). As an upper-division BAS-T course, it is preparatory toward a recognized credential (teaching/ECE certification pathway). Keep CTE classification.

ECED335 Law and Ethics is part of Pierce's BAS-T (Bachelor of Applied Science in Teaching) program with an ECED (Early Childhood Education) prefix, placing it squarely within the Education & Training career cluster (one of the 16 national clusters) and OSPI's Education and Training pathway. The course addresses federal/state special education law (IDEA, FERPA), professional ethics, and advocacy for early learning programs serving birth-grade 3 — these are occupation-specific competencies tied to early childhood education and paraeducator/teacher pathways, not general academic content. Content aligns with industry standards (NAEYC professional standards, WA paraeducator certificate requirements) and supports progression toward recognized credentials (ECE Initial/Short Certificate, paraeducator certification, teacher prep). Professional ethics and family advocacy map to CTE Core Employability Skills applied in real-world educational settings. While the catalog lists Social Sciences distribution, the ECED prefix and BAS-T prerequisite confirm this is a preparatory CTE course within an occupational pathway, not a general-ed humanities transfer course. Lean keep given clear cluster alignment and pathway sequencing.

ECED340 is part of Pierce's BAS-T (Teacher Education) program with prerequisite admission requirement, aligning to the Education & Training career cluster under OSPI's program areas. The course delivers occupation-specific competencies: administering/scoring/interpreting formal assessment tools, developing IEPs/IFSPs/504 plans, and applying legal/ethical provisions for special education placement — these are concrete technical skills used by early childhood educators and special education paraprofessionals. Content is preparatory (occupation-specific) rather than general academic, addresses real-world industry contexts (working with culturally/linguistically/ability-diverse families), and progresses toward a recognized postsecondary credential (BAS-T teaching pathway). This fits Education/Training cluster CTE rather than a general academic transfer course.

ECED355 is part of Pierce's BAS-T (Teaching) program prerequisite sequence, situating it within the Education & Training career cluster (OSPI Human Services / Education pathway). Early Childhood Education is an established CTE preparatory pathway with a documented Curriculum Framework, ties to Washington's Early Achievers/STARS and CDA credential progressions, and aligns with NAEYC professional standards — all CTE-aligned industry standards. Content on cultural responsiveness, identity development, and family/institutional perspectives maps to employability and leadership skills required of early childhood educators working in real-world classroom contexts. While the course also carries a Social Sciences distribution label, its placement in a teacher-prep BAS sequence makes it CTE-appropriate for an Education & Training pathway. Conservative call: keep_target.

Early Childhood Education course within a BAS-T (Bachelor of Applied Science in Teaching) program aligns with the Education & Training career cluster. Content covers professional observation/assessment competencies, IEP/IFSP progress monitoring, and communication strategies — occupation-specific skills for early childhood educators and paraeducators. This is preparatory CTE coursework tied to a recognized postsecondary credential pathway (BAS-T), addressing industry-defined competencies in early childhood education. Fits OSPI's Education & Training cluster and Family & Consumer Sciences / Education program area.

Early Childhood Education course in BAS-T program aligns with Education & Training career cluster. Teaches evidence-based curriculum adaptation, IEP/IFSP/504 lesson planning - occupational competencies for early childhood educators. Progresses toward postsecondary credential (BAS-T) in Education pathway. Fits OSPI Family & Consumer Sciences / Education & Training program area with applied performance assessments (lesson plan creation).

Early Childhood Education course within a BAS-T (Bachelor of Applied Science - Teaching) program aligns with the Education & Training career cluster. Covers occupation-specific pedagogical competencies (language acquisition strategies, literacy instruction, family engagement) for birth-third grade educators, which is preparatory CTE content tied to a recognized teaching credential pathway. Fits OSPI's Education & Training cluster and supports progression toward postsecondary teaching credentials.

Upper-division course in Pierce's BAS-T (Bachelor of Applied Science in Teaching) program focused on early childhood education birth-to-grade-3. Aligns with the Education & Training national career cluster and OSPI's FCS/Education pathway. Content delivers occupational competencies (supervising paraprofessionals/assistants, collaboration with school personnel, community agencies, and families of diverse learners with disabilities) rather than general-ed academic content. Preparatory designation is clear given BAS-T admission prerequisite and progression toward a recognized teaching credential. Real-world workforce application (supervision, interagency collaboration) satisfies leadership/employability skills integration. Not a generic 'Intro to Education' transfer course, which is the cautionary example.

Residency Seminar aligns with Education & Training career cluster (one of 16 national clusters) and OSPI's Family & Consumer Sciences/Education pathway. Content includes occupation-specific competencies: edTPA teaching portfolio (industry-recognized credential pathway), lesson planning, ethical guidelines in teaching, professionalism, advocacy, and reflective practice in real early childhood classrooms with diverse learners. Concurrent residency placement (ECED 497/498) provides preparatory work-based learning with performance assessments (teaching portfolio, observed practice) rather than exams. Integrates employability skills and professional growth planning. Progresses toward recognized credentials (ECE certification, teacher licensure pathway). Meets CTE preparatory criteria for Education & Training cluster.

ECED491 Residency Extension is part of Pierce's BAS-T (Bachelor of Applied Science in Teaching) program and requires students to demonstrate PESB (Professional Educator Standards Board) competencies. This aligns directly with the Education & Training Career Cluster, one of the 16 national clusters. The course covers instructional planning, methodologies, observation/documentation/assessment, and reflective practice — all occupation-specific competencies tied to teaching as a career pathway. PESB alignment is the WA-recognized industry/professional standard for the Education & Training cluster, and the BAS-T context provides clear progression toward a recognized credential (teaching certification). Performance assessments are inherent (demonstrating competencies, implementing lessons). This is preparatory CTE coursework, not general academic content.

ECED497 is a supervised residency/practicum in early childhood education (birth through grade 3), which aligns directly with the Education & Training career cluster under OSPI's CTE framework. It delivers occupation-specific preparatory experience in a real-world setting under a certificated teacher, addresses professionalism, family/community partnerships, child development, and behavior management - all core competencies for the early childhood educator pathway. This matches preparatory CTE designation (occupation-specific work-based learning) rather than general academic transfer. Concurrent enrollment with ECED 485 indicates an integrated pathway sequence toward a recognized ECE credential (e.g., State ECE Initial Certificate / CDA).

Early Childhood Education Special Education Residency aligns with Education & Training career cluster. Hands-on supervised residency in an ECE special education setting delivers occupation-specific competencies, professionalism, and applied employability skills in real-world industry context — core CTE preparatory characteristics. ECE pathway is a recognized OSPI CTE program area (FCS / Education & Training) with progression toward credentials (ECE Initial/Short Certificate, paraeducator, teacher prep). Fits CTE preparatory designation.

ECED&105 aligns with the Education & Training career cluster and OSPI's Family & Consumer Sciences/Education program area. It covers NAEYC Code of Ethical Conduct (industry-defined professional standards), program models, child development theories applied in real-world contexts, and field observations of professionals and programs - all hallmarks of a Preparatory CTE early childhood pathway course that articulates with ECE certificate/AAS programs and supports CDA/state ECE credentials. While the catalog also lists it as a Social Sciences distribution course, the ECE& common course number signals a statewide early-childhood pathway course used in HS CTE Early Childhood Education programs and Educators Rising/FCCLA-aligned sequences.

ECED&107 Health, Safety and Nutrition is a core Early Childhood Education course aligning directly with the Education & Training career cluster (OSPI Family & Consumer Sciences program area). It addresses occupation-specific competencies for child care/early learning professionals: nutrition standards/menu planning, health practices in group care, recognition of abuse/neglect, and mandated reporting responsibilities — all tied to industry standards (WA DCYF/MERIT licensing, CDA credential competency areas). It supports a preparatory pathway toward recognized credentials (CDA, AAS in ECE, state child care licensing) and applied employability skills in real child care settings. This is a textbook CTE course, not academic transfer.

Early Childhood Education practicum aligns with Education & Training career cluster and FCS/Human Services program areas. Applied practicum in an early learning setting with focus on professionalism/ethics, workplace communication, collaborative teamwork, guidance strategies, and relationship-building maps directly to industry competencies for early childhood educators (NAEYC standards) and embeds employability skills in a real-world business/industry context. Practicum format provides performance-based assessment rather than academic-only evaluation. Sequenced with ECED& 105 prerequisite supporting a preparatory pathway toward CDA/AA credential. Clearly CTE, not general academic transfer.

Early Childhood Education (ECED&) courses align directly with the Human Services and Education & Training career clusters under OSPI's Family & Consumer Sciences (FCS) program area. The course covers occupation-specific competencies for infant/toddler caregivers (developmentally appropriate practices, health/safety/nutrition regulations, caregiver role, culturally relevant care), which map to Washington's Early Care and Education pathway and lead toward recognized credentials (Washington State Initial/Short Certificate, CDA). Content is applied/preparatory rather than purely academic, includes industry-standard regulatory knowledge (WAC licensing), and supports FCCLA CTSO integration. Strong fit for CTE.

Aligns with Human Services / FCS cluster (Early Care & Education pathway). Covers WA DEL licensing, business management, health/safety/nutrition, child development - occupation-specific Preparatory CTE content tied to industry standards (WA STARS, DEL licensing) and recognized credentials (Initial Certificate, CDA pathway).

ECED&138 aligns to the Education & Training career cluster and Family & Consumer Sciences (FCS) OSPI program area. Course is occupation-specific (preparatory) for early learning/home visitor workforce, addresses industry competencies (developmental milestones, curriculum planning, home visiting safety, family partnerships, reflective practice) tied to early childhood credentials (WA State Early Achievers/STARS, CDA pathway). Performance-based outcomes (plan and provide home visits, articulate plans) rather than purely academic. Embedded employability skills (family partnerships, reflective practice) applied in real-world contexts. Clear pathway toward postsecondary ECE credential and workforce skills-gap (early learning workforce shortage). Fits cleanly within CTE.

Early Childhood Education Administration aligns with the Human Services career cluster (Early Childhood Development & Services pathway) and OSPI's Family & Consumer Sciences program area. Course delivers occupation-specific competencies: WAC licensing compliance, NAEYC standards, program operations, budgeting, HR practices, and evaluation tools — all directly tied to industry standards (NAEYC) and state regulations. Supports progression toward recognized credentials (ECE State Certificate, Director credentials). Preparatory CTE content, not general-ed.

ECED&160 Curriculum Development aligns with the Education & Training career cluster and Family & Consumer Sciences program area. Early Childhood Education (ECE) coursework is a recognized CTE preparatory pathway tied to the Child Development Associate (CDA) credential and state ECE certificates. Content covers NAEYC Developmentally Appropriate Practices and WA State Early Learning Guidelines (industry/state standards), curriculum planning competencies, and applied program development - all occupational competencies for early childhood educators. This is a clear preparatory CTE course leading to a recognized postsecondary credential.

Early Childhood Education course aligned to Education & Training career cluster and FCS program area. Delivers occupation-specific technical competencies (designing/evaluating early learning environments) grounded in NAEYC industry standards and child development theory. Performance-based assessments (design/evaluation projects), part of WA's ECED& common course numbering sequence leading to recognized credentials (State ECE Certificate, CDA). FCCLA/Educators Rising CTSO alignment available. Clearly preparatory CTE, not general academic transfer.

ECED&180 is an Early Childhood Education course directly aligned to the Education & Training career cluster (OSPI FCS program area). It addresses occupation-specific teaching strategies, developmentally appropriate practice, and culturally relevant pedagogy for birth-age 8 — core competencies in the ECE preparatory pathway leading to recognized credentials (CDA, state ECE certificates). Content covers applied technical skills (phonemic awareness instruction, literacy scaffolding, teacher role) rather than pure academic theory, fitting Preparatory CTE designation. Keep CTE.

ECED&190 is a preparatory CTE course in the Early Childhood Education pathway, aligning to Human Services cluster (Early Childhood Development) and/or Education & Training cluster under OSPI's Family & Consumer Sciences program area. Course content explicitly addresses workplace professionalism/ethics, communication strategies, and collaborative teamwork (CTE Core Employability Skills) applied to real-world child observation contexts. Performance-based assessments (observation data collection, standardized assessment formats, reflection, communicating findings) replace pure exam-based evaluation. The ECED& prefix and EDUC& 115 prerequisite indicate a sequenced career pathway leading to recognized postsecondary credentials (CDA, AA in ECE) and aligns with NAEYC professional standards. FCCLA CTSO integration is standard for FCS/ECE pathways. This is a well-established preparatory CTE course, not an academic-transfer humanities/social-science class.

ECON& 201 Microeconomics is an academic-transfer Social Sciences distribution course covering supply/demand, elasticities, market structures, and government's role in markets. It lacks CTE Curriculum Framework alignment, industry/nationally-defined technical standards (e.g., NAF Finance), Washington CTE Program Standards, leadership/employability skills integration, performance assessments, CIP code/career pathway sequencing, and advisory committee review. The exclusion list explicitly identifies ECON& 201/202 as not CTE absent Finance or Business/Management pathway framing. Fits Social Studies - Elective only.

ECON&202 Macroeconomics is an academic-transfer Social Sciences course covering GDP, inflation, fiscal/monetary policy, and international trade. It lacks Career Cluster alignment, industry-defined technical standards (e.g., NAF Finance), a documented WA CTE Curriculum Framework, integrated Leadership/Employability Skills in real-world business contexts, performance-based assessments, CTSO linkage, and Exploratory/Preparatory CIP designation. Per OSPI guidance, ECON& transfer courses absent Finance or Business/Management pathway framing do not qualify as CTE. It properly fits Social Studies - Elective only.

EMS150 directly supports the Health Science career cluster (Emergency Medical Services pathway) with occupation-specific technical competencies — medical dosage calculation, drug conversions, and medical terminology — that are foundational EMS/paramedic industry skills. This is preparatory CTE content tied to a recognized postsecondary credential pathway (EMT/Paramedic certification), not general academic math. Aligns with Skilled & Technical Sciences / Health Sciences program area.

EMS160 is required for the EMS (Emergency Medical Services) program — a Health Science career cluster pathway with clear occupational/technical preparatory framing toward EMT/paramedic credentialing. This supports CTE classification under Health Sciences program area. However, the content (atoms, molecules, periodic table, bonding, stoichiometry, organic compounds, cell structure/function, mitosis/meiosis) is substantive chemistry and biology with a lab component, satisfying lab science academic content as well. Recommend adding Science (Lab) alongside CTE.
