Course content (staffing, training/development, performance appraisals, compensation, labor relations, EEO compliance, team building) maps directly to SHRM/industry HR competencies and supports occupational/employability skills in a real workforce context. Fits HS CTE Business pathway (potential FBLA/DECA CTSO linkage).

Introduction to Project Management aligns directly with the Business/Management & Administration national Career Cluster and OSPI's Business & Marketing program area. Project management is a recognized occupational competency area with industry-defined standards (PMI/PMBOK, CAPM credential pathway). Course content (project life cycle, priorities, expectations, expense control, reporting, planning/evaluation/control techniques) maps to technical/occupational competencies rather than general academic content. It supports employability skills (planning, communication, resource management) applied in real-world business contexts and progresses toward recognized postsecondary credentials (CAPM/PMP). Fits Preparatory CTE under Business & Marketing pathway; FBLA/DECA CTSO linkage is plausible. Keep as CTE.

BUS256 Statistical Analysis is framed explicitly as statistical techniques for guiding business decisions, placing it in the Business/Management career cluster and OSPI's Business & Marketing program area. The applied business-decision framing (descriptive stats, probability, estimation, hypothesis testing, regression/correlation used for business contexts) distinguishes it from a pure academic transfer statistics course (e.g., MATH& 146) and supports CTE alignment with industry-relevant analytical competencies. Keep as CTE; not adding Math because the course is business-applied rather than a general math credit equivalency without an explicit cross-walk.

Aligns with Marketing and Business/Management clusters (OSPI Business & Marketing program area). Applied social media tools/strategies for workplace use indicates Preparatory occupational competencies, employability skills, and industry-relevant performance application — consistent with HS CTE framing.

Small Business Operations aligns directly with the Business Management & Administration career cluster (one of 16 national clusters) and OSPI's Business & Marketing program area. Course content (small business administration, customer relations, marketing/sales, technology utilization, leading an organization) maps to industry-defined business management competencies. Embedded leadership/employability skills are explicit (leading a small organization, teamwork) and applied in real-world contexts (interacting with the local business community). Performance-based assessment is built in through in-class practice and demonstration rather than solely exams. Strong CTE fit; keep classification.

Career Readiness Skills covers employment search skills and on-the-job employee skills, directly aligning with CTE Core Employability Skills and 21st Century Skills required in all CTE Curriculum Frameworks. The BUS prefix and explicit positioning as preparation for internships (ACCT-290, BUS-290, IT-290) ties it to the Business/Management and IT career clusters under the Business & Marketing OSPI program area. Employability/career readiness courses are a foundational CTE strand, supporting exploratory career exposure and preparatory pathway progression.

Work Internship in the Business department provides supervised hands-on work experience in private/public sector organizations, which directly aligns with the Business/Management career cluster and OSPI's Business & Marketing program area. Internships are a hallmark Preparatory CTE experience: they deliver real-world industry context, embed employability/leadership skills, and produce performance-based assessments tied to workplace competencies. Cooperative work experience courses are routinely approved CTE offerings statewide.

Aligns with Business Management/Administration cluster and OSPI Business & Marketing program area. Covers entrepreneurship, business, finance, marketing, legal, and social media — occupational/technical competencies tied to a recognized career pathway (FBLA/DECA CTSO-linked). Course explicitly addresses "needed technical skills," consistent with CTE preparatory framing rather than general-ed academic content.

Independent Study with description "Study on an individual basis" has no documented Curriculum Framework, no defined career cluster alignment, no industry standards, no leadership/employability skill integration, and no performance assessments tied to OSPI CTE requirements. Content varies entirely by student/instructor agreement, so it cannot be verified as aligned to Business/Management cluster competencies. Best classified as a general Elective.

BUS300 aligns to the Business Management & Administration national career cluster and OSPI's Business & Marketing program area. Course content (management theory, leadership, motivation, team building, change management, group dynamics, power/conflict) maps directly to industry-recognized management competencies and supports CTE Core Leadership and Employability Skills development. Applying theory to real-world business problems supports performance-based learning consistent with CTE expectations. While 300-level signals upper-division academic depth, the subject matter is occupationally focused on management practice, not general-education content, supporting a Preparatory CTE designation tied to a business pathway. Conservatively keep CTE.

BUS310 aligns with the Business Management & Administration career cluster (one of 16 national clusters) and OSPI's Business & Marketing program area. Content covers occupation-specific management competencies (leadership, motivation, team management, conflict resolution, organizational structure, corporate culture) that map to industry-standard management/HR competencies. The course explicitly builds Leadership and Employability Skills (managing teams, resolving conflict, communication, decision-making, professional relationships) applied in real-world organizational contexts — directly matching CTE Core Leadership Skills and Core Employability Skills required by WA CTE Program Standards. "Practical experience" language suggests performance-based application rather than pure academic theory. Course fits a Preparatory designation within a business management pathway with progression toward postsecondary business credentials. Not a general-ed humanities or pure-academic transfer course.

Managerial Accounting aligns directly with OSPI Business & Marketing program area and the Business Management/Finance career clusters. Course delivers occupation-specific competencies (financial statements analysis, budgetary control, performance monitoring, reporting) that map to NBEA accounting standards and HS Business CTE pathways (linked to FBLA/DECA CTSOs). Skills are applied/preparatory in nature rather than general academic - reporting, planning, coordinating, and monitoring organizational performance are real-world business industry competencies. Distinguishable from ECON& 201/202 academic transfer because it teaches technical accounting practice rather than economic theory. Appropriate as CTE classification.

BUS330 aligns with Business/Management and Law/Public Safety career clusters under OSPI's Business & Marketing program area. Content covers business law competencies (contracts, business organizations, employment law, product liability, safety, environmental regulations) directly applicable to occupational practice. The emphasis on managing employees, ethical decision-making, conflict resolution, and preventative law develops employability and leadership skills in real-world business contexts. Course supports preparatory pathway toward business management/administration careers and connects to FBLA/DECA CTSO content areas. Appropriate as CTE.

Financial Management aligns with the Finance career cluster and Business & Marketing OSPI program area. Course delivers occupation-specific competencies (corporate finance, capital management, forecasting, financial decision-making) using applied tools (spreadsheets, analytical methods) consistent with NAF/industry finance standards. Applied/preparatory framing with measurable performance tasks supports CTE classification over pure academic economics.

Fundamentals of Project Management aligns with the Business/Management career cluster and OSPI's Business & Marketing program area. It explicitly references CompTIA Project+ industry certification preparation (a recognized postsecondary credential), and applied project phases function as performance-based competencies in real-world business contexts. This delivers occupational/technical competencies plus employability skills (planning, leading, executing within organizational structures) beyond general academic content, fitting Preparatory CTE framing.

HR Management aligns with Business/Management career cluster and OSPI Business & Marketing program area. Covers occupation-specific competencies (recruiting, selecting, workforce planning) tied to HR industry practice, supports FBLA/DECA CTSO linkage, and addresses employability/leadership skills in real-world organizational contexts. Fits Preparatory CTE designation.

Independent study within the Applied Management BAS program (Business/Management cluster). Students develop learning objectives tied to their business career focus, which aligns with Preparatory CTE work in a real-world business/industry context and supports employability skill development. Conservative call: keep CTE.

BUS400 Economics for Managers is framed as an applied managerial economics course (BUS prefix, "for Managers") rather than a pure ECON& academic-transfer course. Content on market structures, productivity, governmental policy toward business, and global marketplace aligns with the Business/Management and Finance career clusters and OSPI's Business & Marketing program area. As an applied business course it supports preparatory Business Management pathway competencies and employability skills in real-world business contexts. Keeping CTE is defensible; this is not a generic ECON& 201/202 transfer course.

Operations and Logistics aligns directly with two of the 16 national career clusters (Business Management & Administration; Transportation, Distribution & Logistics) and OSPI's Business & Marketing program area. Content covers industry-recognized technical competencies (TQM, JIT, forecasting, inventory theory, supply chain management) that map to professional credentials (APICS/ASCM CPIM, CSCP). The course is occupation-specific rather than general academic transfer, addresses real-world business/industry application, and supports a Preparatory CTE pathway toward operations/logistics/supply chain careers. Fits cleanly within CTE classification.

Digital and Social Media Management aligns directly with the Marketing career cluster and OSPI's Business & Marketing program area. Content covers business objectives, social media strategy, platforms/tactics, and online marketing to reach target customers — these are industry-relevant technical competencies tied to digital marketing standards. Course supports preparatory pathway toward marketing/digital media credentials and connects to DECA/FBLA CTSO activities. Strong fit for HS CTE credit.

Aligns with Business/Management career cluster and OSPI Business & Marketing program area. Covers occupation-relevant strategy concepts, tools, and techniques applicable to real-world business operations, with employability skills embedded in strategic decision-making and corporate social responsibility content. Fits Preparatory CTE framing.

BUS441 Intermediate Project Management aligns with the Business Management & Administration career cluster and OSPI's Business & Marketing program area. The course delivers occupation-specific technical competencies (project planning, monitoring, controlling, cost/quality/performance management) tied to recognized industry standards (PMI/PMBOK framework is the de facto standard for project management credentials such as CAPM/PMP). It integrates leadership and employability skills explicitly (team dynamics, project leadership, team management) applied in real-world business contexts, and uses applied performance tasks via project management software rather than purely academic assessment. This fits a Preparatory CTE designation with a clear pathway toward a recognized postsecondary credential, satisfying the core CTE strands.

Intermediate HR Management aligns directly with the Business Management & Administration national Career Cluster and OSPI's Business & Marketing program area. Content (training/professional development using adult learning theory, compensation/Total Rewards benefit design) maps to recognized HR industry standards (SHRM-CP/PHR competency areas: Learning & Development, Total Rewards), delivers occupation-specific HR competencies (Preparatory), and supports employability skills application in real workforce contexts. FBLA/DECA CTSO linkage is natural. Not a general-ed academic transfer course.

Advanced Project Management aligns to the Business Management & Administration career cluster (OSPI Business & Marketing program area). Content covers industry-recognized PM frameworks (risk/quality management across project lifecycle, Agile methodologies, international project management) tied to PMI/PMBOK standards which map to recognized postsecondary credentials (CAPM/PMP). It delivers occupational/technical competencies in a preparatory pathway with applied real-world business contexts and supports employability/leadership skills (FBLA-aligned). Not a general-academic transfer course.

Advanced HR Management aligns with Business/Management career cluster and OSPI Business & Marketing program area. Covers occupation-specific HR competencies (employment law, compensation, labor relations, EEO, benefits) tied to industry standards (SHRM/HRCI). Preparatory-level course supporting HR pathway with employability skills and real-world business context. Fits CTE classification.

Capstone in project management aligns to Business Management & Administration cluster (OSPI Business & Marketing program area). Course delivers occupation-specific leadership/change management competencies tied to PMI/industry project management standards, with applied real-world organizational design practice — meets Preparatory CTE criteria.

Capstone HR Management course aligns clearly with the Business Management & Administration career cluster (OSPI Business & Marketing program area). Content covers occupation-specific HR competencies: legal/regulatory compliance, recruitment/selection, performance management, and compensation/benefits — all industry-recognized HR functions (SHRM-aligned). Capstone format with case-study evaluation provides performance-based assessment beyond exams, and integrates prior coursework toward a preparatory HR pathway. Fits CTE Preparatory designation with progression toward postsecondary HR credentials. Not a general-ed academic transfer course.

Applied Management Internship aligns with Business/Management career cluster and OSPI Business & Marketing program area. Supervised, evaluated work-based learning in an actual industry setting directly delivers Core Employability Skills, Leadership Skills, and 21st Century Skills in real-world business contexts — a hallmark CTE preparatory experience. Performance assessment is based on documented hands-on learning rather than exams/essays, satisfying CTE performance-assessment requirements. Internships are a standard capstone in CTE business pathways (FBLA-aligned) and progress toward postsecondary/workforce credentials. Keep as CTE.

CMST& 220 is a general-education public speaking transfer course focused on extemporaneous oral presentations, ethical research, logical analysis, and organization of informative/persuasive speeches. The description shows no alignment to any of the 16 national Career Clusters (no Business/Marketing, no Arts/AV/Communications media-production framing), no industry/nationally-defined technical standards, no Washington State CTE Program Standards crosswalk, no documented Curriculum Framework, no Exploratory/Preparatory CIP designation, no CTSO linkage, and no progression toward a recognized postsecondary credential or certification. The OSPI guidance explicitly calls out CMST& 220 as a course that "sounds like CTE but is NOT" when taught as general-ed speech without business/marketing or media-production cluster alignment — which matches this description exactly. The ELA classification is appropriate because the course emphasizes ethical research, critical/logical analysis, and organization of communication — core ELA competencies aligned with speaking/listening and rhetorical analysis strands.

Community Health Navigation and Care Coordination aligns with the Health Science career cluster (specifically Health Informatics / Support Services pathway) and overlaps with Human Services cluster. Care navigation, coordination, and patient/client advocacy are recognized occupational roles (Community Health Worker, Patient Navigator, Care Coordinator) with industry competencies defined by organizations like NCHEC and the Patient Advocate Certification Board. The course is occupation-specific (Preparatory) rather than general academic, addresses real-world health/human services contexts, and supports progression toward recognized credentials (e.g., CHW certification). Keep as CTE.

CHP260 Global Health aligns with the Health Science career cluster — it prepares students to serve as clinicians, educators, and researchers in global health contexts. The course addresses culturally competent healthcare delivery, comparative healthcare systems, and community health practice, which are occupational competencies relevant to the Health Sciences OSPI program area. Field work component suggests applied/performance-based assessment rather than purely academic study. While it has academic dimensions (comparative analysis of cultures and systems), the explicit framing around healthcare delivery and clinical/educator/researcher roles ties it to a recognized CTE pathway. Conservative call: keep as CTE.

CHP300 is an upper-division academic-transfer community/population health theory course exploring social determinants, health disparities, and biomedical/political/economic aspects of community health. The description shows no Health Science cluster industry standards (no NHA, NCHSE, OSHA credentials), no clinical/technical skill demonstrations, no leadership/employability skills integration, no CTSO linkage (HOSA), no Exploratory/Preparatory pathway designation, and no advisory-committee workforce justification. Content is theoretical public-health analysis, not occupational/technical competencies. Fits HS Health credit (community health, wellness, disparities, burnout/compassion fatigue) and arguably Science (Non-Lab) for the biomedical/epidemiological content, but does not meet OSPI CTE Curriculum Framework requirements.

Community Health Advocacy aligns with the Health Science career cluster - covers chronic health screenings, health insurance/healthcare system navigation, confidentiality protocols, case management, mental health first aid, and community/population health advocacy. These are occupation-specific competencies tied to community health worker (CHW) and patient navigator roles, which are recognized credentials in WA's Health Sciences program area. Content is applied/preparatory rather than purely academic transfer, supporting Health Science cluster alignment under OSPI CTE standards.

Course aligns with Health Science cluster (Community Health Practitioner program) and IT cluster overlap. Covers health informatics — data collection/management, health communication across audiences/media, and strategic information design — all occupational competencies for public health/health informatics careers. Maps to OSPI Health Sciences program area with applied technical skills (data management, multimedia communication, audience-targeted messaging) rather than general academic content. Performance-based design ("ability to collect, gather, present, design") supports CTE preparatory framing toward health informatics/community health worker pathways.

CHP315 Health Policy, Law, and Ethics is an upper-division academic public health policy course examining healthcare laws, ethical frameworks, and population vs. individual rights. It lacks the required CTE components: no Health Science cluster technical/clinical competencies, no industry certifications (e.g., CNA, MA, EMT), no documented Curriculum Framework tied to WA CTE Program Standards, no integrated Leadership/Employability Skills in real-world clinical/industry contexts, no performance assessments via labs or technical skill demonstrations, no CTSO linkage (HOSA), and no Exploratory/Preparatory designation with CIP code. It is academic policy analysis, not occupational/technical training. The content best fits Civics (public policy, law, individual rights vs. government) and Health (community health, disease prevention, healthy lifestyles).

Introduction to Epidemiology aligns with the Health Science career cluster (population health, disease characterization), fitting CTE under Health Sciences program area, especially with applied case studies, team activities, and projects that support employability skills. However, the course is also fundamentally a population-health science course covering scientific concepts and methods (epidemiologic reasoning, health data analysis) without a wet lab component, so it also satisfies Science (Non-Lab) credit. Keeping CTE while adding Science (Non-Lab) reflects the dual nature of applied Health Science CTE coursework with scientific content rigor.

Course aligns with Health Science career cluster and OSPI Health Sciences program area. Covers population health, prevention, patient education, behavior change, and chronic disease recognition - all occupationally-relevant competencies for healthcare pathways. Content supports preparatory health career sequences (nursing, public health, community health worker). While the description does not explicitly cite WA CTE Program Standards, CTSO (HOSA) linkage, or performance assessments, the applied health-industry content fits CTE Health Sciences framing more than academic Science. The 300-level designation suggests upper-division health professions content rather than general-ed academic transfer. Conservative keep given clear Health Science cluster alignment.

Program Planning and Evaluation aligns with the Health Science career cluster, focusing on developing, implementing, and evaluating health promotion programs in community/healthcare settings. It addresses occupational competencies (evidence-based program models, data collection methods, logistics/resource coordination) directly tied to public health and healthcare administration pathways. Content involves applied real-world industry context (community healthcare delivery) rather than pure academic transfer. Reasonable fit for CTE Health Sciences program area; keep classification.

Healthcare Research Methods aligns with the Health Science career cluster, teaching occupation-specific research competencies (hypothesis development, variable specification, validity/reliability testing, research design) used in clinical and applied healthcare settings. Content supports preparatory pathway progression toward healthcare credentials and research roles, with applied/performance-based work (designing research projects) consistent with CTE assessment expectations. While the course has academic rigor, its explicit healthcare framing distinguishes it from a generic statistics/research-methods transfer course, keeping it within the Health Sciences OSPI program area.

Disaster Preparedness aligns with the Law, Public Safety, Corrections & Security national Career Cluster (Emergency Management pathway) under OSPI's Skilled & Technical Sciences program area. Course content directly maps to industry competencies: threat assessment, risk analysis, EOC staffing, interagency coordination (NIMS/ICS framework), and incident management - all recognized emergency management technical standards (FEMA/EMI aligned). Includes applied/preparatory occupational skills (formulating response plans, testing programs, follow-up analysis) rather than purely academic content, supporting career progression toward emergency management certifications. Fits CTE preparatory designation with measurable performance components (plan formulation, EOC simulation).

CHP360 Global Health is an upper-division applied health course preparing students as clinicians, educators, and researchers serving global populations. It aligns with the Health Science career cluster (OSPI Health Sciences program area), addresses culturally competent care delivery, community health practice, and includes field work — consistent with CTE preparatory framing toward healthcare occupations. Although academic in tone, the explicit clinician/educator/researcher pathway language and applied field component support CTE classification over generic Science. Lean keep_target as marginal.

Community Health Independent Study is a generic, student-designed independent learning project with no documented curriculum framework, no industry/technical standards alignment, no defined CTE program area (Health Sciences pathway requires structured competencies), no leadership/employability skills integration, no performance assessments tied to industry credentials, and no CTSO linkage (e.g., HOSA). The vague "jointly designed by student and instructor" framing cannot meet OSPI's requirement for documented alignment to Washington State CTE Program Standards, a specific Career Cluster, or a Curriculum Framework with measurable performance assessments. Best classified as a general Elective.

CHP400 Environmental Health is an academic public/environmental health survey covering water/air quality, food safety, waste disposal, occupational health, disease agents, toxic exposure, and regulatory policy. It is conceptual content delivery, not a CTE course: no documented Curriculum Framework, no industry-defined technical standards (e.g., no HAZWOPER, EPA cert pathway, NEHA Registered Sanitarian competencies), no Leadership/Employability skills integration, no performance/lab-based technical skill demonstrations, no CTSO linkage, no Exploratory/Preparatory designation tied to a CIP/career pathway, and no clear progression to a recognized credential or apprenticeship. The content fits a Science (Non-Lab) credit — environmental science survey with policy/regulatory framing — rather than CTE Health Science or Skilled & Technical Sciences clusters, which require occupation-specific competencies and applied skills.

Course covers public health and social-ecological models of trauma — academic public health content without CTE Health Science cluster framing. No industry-defined technical standards (e.g., no clinical/medical certification path), no documented WA CTE Curriculum Framework, no leadership/employability skills integration, no performance/lab skill demonstrations, no CTSO (HOSA) linkage, and no skills-gap/advisory-committee justification. Description is conceptual/theoretical (identify risk factors, influences) — fits HS Health credit (community/public health content) rather than CTE Health Sciences preparatory pathway.

Course aligns with Human Services career cluster (family/community services pathway) and addresses health/wellness across five domains (physical, emotional, psychological, intellectual, spiritual), which also satisfies HS Health credit requirements. Family systems content supports FCS program area within CTE. Conservatively retaining CTE while adding Health since the five-areas-of-health framing directly maps to WA Health standards.

CHP430 Epidemics and Prevention examines epidemiology concepts, public health policy, emergency preparedness, and government/NGO response roles. While health-adjacent, the description shows no occupational/technical competencies tied to a Health Science career cluster pathway (no clinical skills, no industry credentials like CNA/MA/EMT, no NCHSE standards, no performance-based skill demonstrations, no CTSO/HOSA framing, no Washington CTE Curriculum Framework alignment). It is an academic public-health/policy survey course suited to non-lab science credit (health/policy science content) rather than CTE Health Sciences which requires occupation-specific technical skills and employability competencies in real-world clinical/industry contexts.

Course aligns with Health Science career cluster as community/public health content. Description references community health work, cultural competency for health practitioners, and addressing health disparities - all applied workforce-relevant skills for the Health Sciences program area. While it leans more theoretical than clinical/lab-based, cultural competency is a documented industry competency for healthcare workers and aligns with employability skills in real-world health contexts. Marginal but defensible as CTE given Health Sciences pathway scaffolding; lean keep_target per conservative rule.

Capstone for Community Health Professional BAS concentration aligns with Health Science career cluster (OSPI Health Sciences program area). Capstone format inherently includes performance assessment and real-world industry application, supporting occupational/technical competencies toward a recognized postsecondary credential (BAS). Preparatory CTE designation fits.

EMS Ethics and Leadership aligns with the Health Science career cluster and Skilled & Technical Sciences program area. It is occupation-specific (EMS) and addresses CTE Core Leadership Skills and Employability Skills (ethics, leadership, organizational climate) in a real-world industry context, supporting progression toward EMS credentials/certifications.

Emergency Management aligns with the Law, Public Safety, Corrections & Security national career cluster, which is one of the 16 OSPI-recognized CTE clusters. The course covers occupation-specific content (vocabulary, core components, leadership/management roles in emergency response) tied to a growing workforce field, consistent with a Preparatory CTE designation under Skilled & Technical Sciences. Content addresses real-world industry context (man-made, natural, technological disasters) and management/leadership skills aligned to employability standards. This is not a general-education academic transfer course; it maps to a recognized career pathway with postsecondary/credential progression (FEMA/IAEM certifications). Conservative call: keep CTE.

CHPM420 Injury Prevention is an upper-division academic health promotion survey course covering major issues in health promotion, premature mortality/morbidity causes, behavioral/environmental contributions to illness, risk reduction strategies, and economic/policy/ethical issues. It reads as a public health overview rather than an OSPI-approved CTE Health Sciences course. There is no evidence of: (1) alignment to a specific Career Cluster with industry/nationally-defined technical standards (e.g., HOSA competencies, clinical skills, CNA/EMT prep), (2) WA CTE Program Standards documented in a Curriculum Framework, (3) Leadership/Employability Skills integration, (4) performance assessments such as labs/clinical skill demonstrations, (5) Exploratory/Preparatory designation tied to a CIP code, or (6) progression toward a recognized postsecondary credential or certification. The content aligns with the HS Health credit strand (health promotion, disease/injury prevention, risk reduction, wellness behaviors) under the WA Health & Fitness K-12 Learning Standards rather than CTE.

Community Paramedicine is an advanced paramedic specialty course that aligns directly with the Health Science career cluster under OSPI's Health Sciences program area. It delivers occupation-specific technical competencies (health assessments, chronic disease monitoring, medication management, immunizations, lab specimen collection, minor medical procedures) tied to a recognized paramedic credential pathway. This is preparatory CTE content with clear industry standards (EMS/paramedic scope of practice), real-world clinical application, and progression toward a postsecondary credential. Not a general-ed academic transfer course.

Community Paramedicine Internship aligns with the Health Science career cluster under OSPI's Health Sciences program area. Clinical internships deliver occupation-specific (Preparatory) competencies tied to EMS/paramedicine industry standards (NREMT, state EMS certification), inherently integrate employability skills in real-world healthcare contexts, and use performance-based assessment (clinical skill demonstrations). Progresses toward recognized credential. Classic CTE Health Sciences placement.

Critical Care Transport prepares paramedics for advanced clinical care and leads to recognized industry certifications (FP-C, CCP-C via IBSC). Aligns directly with Health Science career cluster, delivers occupation-specific technical competencies, performance-based clinical assessments, and progression to postsecondary credentials — core CTE Preparatory criteria.

Critical Care Transport Internship aligns squarely with the Health Science career cluster under OSPI's Health Sciences program area. Hands-on ICU clinical rotations are performance-based assessments tied to occupational/technical competencies (preparatory designation), directly supporting progression toward recognized healthcare credentials (paramedic/critical care transport certifications). This is exactly the type of applied, industry-standard clinical training CTE is designed to capture.

EMS capstone in critical care/community paramedic/EMS leadership aligns to Health Science cluster, Health Sciences program area, with clinical performance assessments and industry credential pathway (NREMT/paramedic). Preparatory CTE.

Tobacco/nicotine treatment training aligns to Health Science career cluster — occupational competencies in tobacco dependence treatment, pharmacologic management, counseling techniques, and motivational interviewing are industry-specific clinical skills tied to recognized tobacco treatment specialist credentials (e.g., NCTTP/CTTS). Course is preparatory occupational training, not general academic content.

Course aligns with Health Science career cluster and OSPI Health Sciences program area. Focuses on occupation-specific leadership/supervision competencies for healthcare environments, integrating leadership and employability skills (a required CTE strand) applied in real-world industry context. Preparatory designation tied to healthcare pathway. Not a general-ed academic course.

Course teaches healthcare professionals how to provide peer, student, and client education — directly aligned with Health Science career cluster (patient/client education is a core occupational competency for healthcare workers). Applied/preparatory framing for working healthcare professionals fits CTE's occupational skill-building criteria rather than general academic content. Conservative call: keep CTE.

Advanced Patient Care aligns directly with the Health Science career cluster and OSPI's Health Sciences program area. It delivers occupation-specific (Preparatory) competencies for Respiratory Care Practitioners in adult critical care, neonatal/pediatric critical care, and emergency settings, with explicit preparation for recognized industry credentials (NBRC adult critical care and neonatal/pediatric specialty credentials). Content is tied to industry/nationally defined technical standards for respiratory therapy, includes applied clinical/critical-thinking performance demonstrations rather than solely exams, and supports progression toward postsecondary credentials—satisfying core CTE strands. Properly classified as CTE.

Advanced Respiratory Care Capstone is a clinical capstone in Health Science cluster (Skilled & Technical Sciences / Health Sciences program area). It delivers occupation-specific competencies in respiratory therapy specialties (critical care, neonatal, pediatrics) with performance-based clinical assessments, aligning to preparatory CTE pathway toward an industry credential (RRT/CRT).

Patient Care & Radiation Safety is a CT (computed tomography) imaging course aligned to the Health Science career cluster. It covers occupation-specific competencies (patient assessment, monitoring, infection control, CT dose management, radiation safety) tied to ARRT/industry standards for radiologic technologists, fitting OSPI's Health Sciences program area as Preparatory CTE coursework progressing toward a recognized postsecondary credential.

Computed Tomography image production aligns to the Health Science career cluster with industry-specific technical competencies (CT console parameters, PACS, DICOM, networking, archiving) tied to ARRT/medical imaging credentials. This is occupation-specific preparatory content under Health Sciences program area, not general academics.

CT282 Cross Sectional Anatomy & Pathophysiology is a Computed Tomography (CT) specialty course clearly aligned to the Health Science career cluster under OSPI's Health Sciences program area. The course prefix "CT" and explicit reference to "pathologies...applicable to Computed Tomography" indicate this is part of a CT/medical imaging preparatory pathway leading to ARRT (American Registry of Radiologic Technologists) credentialing - a recognized postsecondary industry certification. Content covers occupation-specific technical competencies (sectional anatomy recognition in imaging planes, pathology identification) required for radiologic technologists/CT technologists, not general academic anatomy. This satisfies industry-defined technical standards (ARRT/JRCERT), occupational preparatory designation, and progression toward a recognized credential. Conservative call: keep_target.

Procedures for Computed Tomography is a preparatory occupation-specific course in the Health Science career cluster (Diagnostic Services pathway). It teaches industry-specific technical competencies (CT protocols, contrast media administration, sectional anatomy, patient care) aligned to ARRT/JRCERT national standards for CT technologists. Content directly supports a recognized postsecondary credential (ARRT CT certification) and progression toward a regulated allied-health occupation. This is a clear Skilled & Technical Sciences / Health Sciences CTE fit, not general academic content.

Computed Tomography Lab & Clinical Introduction is a hands-on occupational/technical course aligned to the Health Science career cluster (OSPI Health Sciences program area). Students learn specific industry equipment (Phillips CT scanner console, gantry, data acquisition), patient positioning protocols, scan procedures for Head/Chest/Abdomen, and IV start skills on prosthetic arms — all clear preparatory CT technologist competencies tied to ARRT/JRCERT industry standards. Lab-based performance assessments (skill demonstrations) rather than exams. This is a textbook Preparatory CTE course leading toward a recognized postsecondary credential (CT certification), so CTE classification is correct.

Registry review for ARRT Computed Tomography certification exam - directly aligns with Health Science cluster, Health Sciences program area, preparatory designation, and recognized postsecondary/industry credential (ARRT). Occupation-specific technical content, not general academic.

Clinical CT (Computed Tomography) practicum aligns with Health Science career cluster under OSPI's Health Sciences program area. Delivers occupation-specific technical competencies through 20 hrs/week clinical placement in a CT department - classic Preparatory CTE with performance-based skill demonstrations toward a recognized postsecondary credential (ARRT CT certification). Hands-on industry-site competency development is exactly what HS CTE Health Sciences requires.

Clinical CT (Computed Tomography) practicum aligns to Health Science cluster, Skilled & Technical Sciences program area. Preparatory occupation-specific course with performance-based competency development in a real-world clinical setting (20 hrs/week), tied to a recognized postsecondary credential (ARRT CT certification). Matches CTE strands: industry-standard technical competencies, performance assessment via clinical skill demonstration, workforce pathway alignment.

Computed Tomography independent study sits within the Health Science cluster (medical imaging / Skilled & Technical Sciences program area).
